item.title: Burying the Last Comfortable Illusion About China
item.description:
Burying the Last Comfortable Illusion About China

The first myth fell years ago: that China cannot innovate. Dan Wang, author of Breakneck: China's Quest to Engineer the Future, considers that one buried and is glad for it. The second myth is now on life support: that China can scale but not originate — that the zero-to-one creative leap belongs exclusively to the West, and the Chinese are merely excellent at going from one to a hundred. Wang wants to kill this one too, and he brings receipts.

"Numbers are continuous," he says, with the blunt patience of someone who has made this point seventy-odd times on a book tour. The distinction between innovation and scaling is a Western security blanket, not an empirical observation. Bell Labs invented solar panels in New Jersey in 1957. Bell Labs no longer exists. China owns the entire solar supply chain — polysilicon processing, wafer production, module assembly, and the equipment to make all of it. The zero-to-one inventors lost to the one-to-a-hundred scalers so completely that the original invention is irrelevant. "What does it matter," Wang asks, "if you go from zero to one, if you cannot go from one to a hundred?"

DeepSeek, EVs, factory robotics, semiconductor manufacturing equipment — the pattern keeps repeating. Wang estimates roughly half of the serious people he spoke with on his book tour are still clinging to the zero-to-one distinction. He finds this increasingly hard to defend. "It starts sounding a little bit absurd. They can make all the toys and t-shirts, and they can make the large-capacity batteries for the EVs, but they can't..." The sentence doesn't need finishing.

Engineers vs. Lawyers: A Framework That Went Viral

The conceit that made Wang's book a Financial Times Best Book of 2025 — and that Adam Tooze was already using in London Review of Books keynotes without attribution — is the distinction between the engineering state and the lawyerly society. China is governed by engineers trained to execute. The United States is governed by lawyers trained to argue.

Wang is careful to note the idea wasn't original to him. Bill Clinton quipped about it in 1996 standing in China. But what Wang did was weaponize it — turn it into a framework sharp enough to actually explain how China's government operates and why, and how America's dysfunction looks from the outside. His preparation was almost absurdly perfect: years in Beijing, Hong Kong, and Shanghai analyzing Chinese semiconductor and manufacturing companies for Gavekal Research under Arthur Kroeber; then living through zero-COVID, a policy whose ambition was right there in the name; then moving immediately to a fellowship at Yale Law School, embedded among the people who would staff the Biden administration. Jake Sullivan. Gina Raimondo. Brian Dees. All Yale Law.

The contrast was visceral. Zero-COVID had the mathematical clarity of a missile engineer's optimization function — one of China's foundational zero-COVID architects was indeed a cybernetics expert. The lawyers at Yale operated differently. They were trained to make the best argument for whatever position they needed to occupy.

When host Steve Hsu pushes back — shouldn't scientists and engineers be frustrated with lawyers rather than deferential? — Wang flips the challenge. "Rather than decrying the lawyers for having seized all that power for themselves, I would say, why don't we point the finger at the economists, who had power tenuously, but then gave it up... Why don't we say to the engineers and the scientists, can you not get better at organizing society?" Power, as Nancy Pelosi once noted, is not given. It must be seized.

Wang is not naive about the engineering state's failures. Zero-COVID was a perfect expression of technocratic logic followed to a catastrophic conclusion — 25 million people locked in Shanghai because the math said zero. The One Child Policy was similarly the product of engineering-state thinking. Wang's prescription is pluralism: "What we want is pluralism. What we need are some scientists in the ruling elites, we need economists, lawyers, humanists." The problem with America is not the presence of lawyers. It is the near-total absence of everyone else.

The Industrial Party: An Online Meme That Became State Policy

Sometime in the early 2010s, before the Great Firewall had fully clamped down, a loose movement emerged on the Chinese internet. It had no party affiliation — there are only eight tolerated political parties in China, all subservient to the Communist Party, and this was not one of them. It was more of a meme, a current of thought advocating technocratic governance aimed at restoring Chinese scientific and industrial supremacy.

The Industrial Party, as it came to be known, produced canonical texts. One was called "Study or Wash Dishes." Another, "The Morning Star of Linggao," was online fan fiction imagining present-day people sent back to the Ming Dynasty to industrialize the island of Hainan. But Wang argues the deepest canonical text was Liu Cixin's The Three-Body Problem — humanity banding together under technocratic rule, building enormous navy-controlled spaceships to confront an existential extraterrestrial threat. That is the Industrial Party's political fantasy made literary.

Wang sees this worldview as deeply rooted in China's reading of its own history. For centuries, China understood itself as the preeminent civilization. Then, in a period it calls the Century of Humiliation, technologically superior barbarians — British, Dutch, eventually Japanese — arrived and inflicted extraordinary damage. The lesson absorbed: science and technology are what the barbarians used to beat us, and science and technology are what we must master to reclaim our place. This story, Wang notes, was told before the Communist Party and has been amplified by it into the bones of every Chinese person.

Wang offers a sharp counter-note: "What about the quarter century of self-humiliation that the Communist Party inflicted right afterwards?" The Great Leap Forward. The Cultural Revolution. The One Child Policy. The Strike Hard campaigns. These were not inflicted by barbarians. They emerged organically from within. The Industrial Party narrative conveniently omits this chapter.

The Ferocity No One Talks About

One of Wang's most striking claims, delivered almost as an aside: China embodies greater capitalist competition and greater capitalist excess than America does today. This from a country that still officially espouses Marxism.

American tech, Wang observes, is something like a gentleman's club. Google has search. Amazon has e-commerce. Facebook has social. They don't tread aggressively on each other's territory. Peter Thiel's famous dictum — competition is for losers, monopoly is the goal — is essentially the operating philosophy of Silicon Valley's biggest incumbents, and their political influence is sufficient to ensure the government mostly leaves them alone.

In China, everyone is fighting everyone over everything. Kyle Chan's chart of interlocking competitive relationships among Chinese tech companies looks like a circuit diagram from a nightmare. The result: profit margins driven toward zero, consumers with extraordinary buying power, and a Shanghai Composite index that has traded sideways for twenty years. Bad for Chinese equity investors. Spectacular for Chinese consumers. A wealthy American venture investor living in Beijing told Hsu he simply cannot replicate his quality of life in the United States, even as a billionaire. The food delivery, the services, the sheer purchasing power — none of it maps to what money buys in California.

The irony Wang highlights: China arrests Marxist labor organizers. There are no real unions. The workers have no protection from the relentless competitive pressure the party deliberately fosters. "What sort of socialism is this?"

Preparing Without Eagerness, Waging Without Preparing

Wang has a quote — one of his most cited — that he attributes to Viennese satirist Karl Kraus: "In Berlin, the situation is serious but not hopeless. Whereas in Vienna, the situation is hopeless but it is not serious." He applies this to the US-China cold war. Beijing has been preparing for Cold War without eagerness for waging it. Washington wants to wage a Cold War without preparing for it. America is Vienna.

China grew extraordinarily wealthy under the American-built liberal international order — becoming a major maritime trading power without building a large navy, which Wang notes may be historically unprecedented. Britain, Portugal, the Netherlands, the United States — all built navies to become trading powers. China got the trading power for free, underwritten by American security guarantees. That system is now turning against China. The Chinese are reacting, not initiating.

Meanwhile China has been quietly preparing for the siege: the Mayor's Vegetable Basket Program ensures farmland rings every major city for food self-sufficiency. Energy self-sufficiency is a stated national priority. Technological self-sufficiency — the semiconductor drive — was accelerated precisely by American export controls. Wang has been openly critical of those controls for years, writing in the New York Times and Foreign Affairs that they would ultimately stimulate Chinese self-sufficiency rather than prevent it. He appears to have been right.

The American failure, as Wang diagnoses it, is an unwillingness to pay the high costs required to actually compete. Reshoring manufacturing, rebuilding industrial capacity, funding science — these require sustained generational commitments. "America is not a serious country in a lot of different ways," he says, not cruelly but clinically.

Authoritarianism and Science: A More Complicated Relationship

One of the cherished beliefs of the lawyerly society is that political freedom is a prerequisite for scientific innovation. Free speech enables the productive collision of ideas. Suppression stifles breakthroughs. Wang interrogates this and finds it considerably more complicated.

He cites Harvard historian of science Lauren Graham, who studied the Soviet system and found something counterintuitive: the two most catastrophic events for Soviet scientists were the Stalinist purges of the 1930s and the dissolution of the Soviet Union in the 1990s. Which was worse? The dissolution. When the Soviet Union fell, the money stopped. Labs closed. Scientists emigrated or grew potatoes to survive. During the Stalin purges, by contrast, scientists staggered out of gulags and produced Nobel Prize-winning work, because Stalin funded science lavishly. "When you throw a lot of money at science, you're probably going to get a lot of science."

Hsu corroborates this from direct experience: Soviet mathematicians and physicists who emigrated to the US after 1991 were uniformly nostalgic for the closed system. Secret cities adjacent to weapons programs, where scientists wanted for nothing — food, salaries, orchestras, chess — in exchange for avoiding topics like the Ukrainian wheat harvest. The constraint was narrow. Within it, complete intellectual freedom. Hsu found their seminars ran for hours, fighting to the bottom of every question.

Wang will not say authoritarianism is good for science. What he will say: money is good for science. The Trump administration is cutting scientific funding. University budgets are contracting. China is funding science aggressively. "I think they're going to get the science."

The deeper point is structural. A physicist obsessed with improving a language model's attention mechanism is not thinking about politics. He is thinking about the problem. He can do that in Beijing as readily as in Mountain View — and in Beijing, unlike at Stanford, he can likely get direct access to frontier model training because Chinese universities and companies are more tightly integrated than their American counterparts.

Automation, AI, and the Danger of Magical Thinking

There is a fantasy circulating in American policy circles: that AI and automation will close the gap with China's industrial machine without requiring the hard work of actually rebuilding industrial capacity. Wang is not buying it.

Chinese factories are, on average, ahead on automation. Chinese Tesla workers are more productive than California Tesla workers. China installs as many industrial robots per year as the rest of the world combined. And China has massive advantages in AI training data from its manufacturing operations. The idea that American AI investments — currently approaching 2% of GDP in data center and compute CapEx — will magically solve the robotics gap, the port automation gap, the assembly line gap, strikes Wang as a category error. "We have to be careful not to let automation, like superintelligence, become an excuse for magical thinking rather than doing the hard work of capacity."

On the longer-horizon AI question — recursive self-improvement, existential risk — Wang is neither dismissive nor apocalyptic. He believes the recursive inflection point is real but not proximate, probably five to ten years out. He has participated in war games at the Tate Modern, led the China team against Stanley McChrystal's US team, and watched as both sides, facing the recursive moment, negotiated slowdowns and mutual lab inspections. That outcome gave him some optimism — not because it solves the problem, but because it demonstrates the possibility of the narrative.

He notes that Dario Amodei and Demis Hassabis, at Davos, both said they are in communication and watching for the recursive inflection point together, and both expressed a preference for slower timelines. They would slow down if they could. The problem is collective action: it only takes one lab — one nation, one billionaire with a data center in the UAE — to defect from any slowdown agreement. "It's a little bit like global warming."

Wang ends with a point that loops back to the lawyers-versus-engineers frame. Scientists and engineers need to get better at narrative. The war game at the Tate Modern produced an optimistic ending precisely because narrative matters — because the ape brain responds to enacted story in a way it does not respond to technical arguments. "We are all ruled by ape brains. And so this is why the lawyers are in charge. And the scientists and engineers need to get better at narrative."

What To Do With This

  • Retire the zero-to-one / one-to-a-hundred framework when assessing Chinese competition. It is now empirically indefensible and serves mainly to produce complacency in Western strategy and investment.
  • Track where elite Chinese AI researchers are choosing to work, and ask why. The 90% retention figure in the US is real but may not hold; the Beijing academic-industry integration is a genuine structural advantage that could shift talent flows.
  • Study the Industrial Party's canonical texts — especially The Three-Body Problem as political philosophy, not just science fiction — to understand the motivating worldview of Chinese technocrats and the engineers who build for them.
  • Assess any American AI or automation investment against the specific industrial gaps China leads in (port automation, factory robotics, EV supply chain) rather than assuming general AI progress closes those gaps automatically.
  • Evaluate US semiconductor export controls against their actual effect: Wang's long-standing argument — that they stimulate Chinese self-sufficiency rather than preventing it — is tracking as correct, and strategy should update accordingly.
  • When evaluating scientific productivity claims about authoritarian systems, ask whether the constraint is narrow or totalizing. Soviet scientists worked well inside a narrow constraint; the analogy to China is not perfect but is closer than Western commentators typically acknowledge.
  • Take Chinese consumer market purchasing power seriously as a talent retention tool. Quality of life for high earners in Chinese cities, driven by ferocious service competition, is a real factor in decisions about where to live and work.
  • Do not treat AI as a substitute for industrial policy. Capacity building in manufacturing, logistics, and physical automation requires sustained multi-decade investment regardless of what language models can do. Wang's warning against "magical thinking" is the most actionable policy insight in the conversation.
  • Build or support channels for AI lab coordination across geopolitical lines now, before the recursive inflection point. The collective action problem becomes much harder to solve at speed; the infrastructure for cooperation should be established in advance.

Lines Worth Keeping

"I want to suggest, no, let's bury this idea too, that the Chinese are able to both innovate as well as scale, that numbers are continuous. This idea of going from zero to one as just exclusively the remit of the West — I think is not empirical at this point."

— Dan Wang
"What does it matter if you go from zero to one, if you cannot go from one to a hundred?"

— Dan Wang
"Probably the most underrated part of the Chinese system is the ferocity of market competition. It's excusable not to see that, given that the party espouses so much Marxism. I would argue that China embodies both greater capitalist competition and greater capitalist excess than America does today."

— Dan Wang
"Beijing has been preparing for Cold War without eagerness for waging it. The US wants to wage a Cold War without preparing for it."

— Dan Wang
"Rather than decrying the lawyers for having seized all that power for themselves, why don't we say to the engineers and the scientists: can you not get better at organizing society, can you not make a case better to the population, and try to get that power away from them?"

— Dan Wang
"Which event was worse for Soviet scientists — the Stalin purges or the dissolution of the Soviet Union? Actually, it was the dissolution. Because that was when the money stopped."

— Dan Wang, citing Harvard historian Lauren Graham
"We are all ruled by ape brains. And so this is why the lawyers are in charge. And the scientists and engineers need to get better at narrative."

— Dan Wang
"We have to be careful not to let automation, like superintelligence, become an excuse for magical thinking rather than doing the hard work of capacity."

— Dan Wang
item.content:encoded:
Burying the Last Comfortable Illusion About China

The first myth fell years ago: that China cannot innovate. Dan Wang, author of Breakneck: China's Quest to Engineer the Future, considers that one buried and is glad for it. The second myth is now on life support: that China can scale but not originate — that the zero-to-one creative leap belongs exclusively to the West, and the Chinese are merely excellent at going from one to a hundred. Wang wants to kill this one too, and he brings receipts.

"Numbers are continuous," he says, with the blunt patience of someone who has made this point seventy-odd times on a book tour. The distinction between innovation and scaling is a Western security blanket, not an empirical observation. Bell Labs invented solar panels in New Jersey in 1957. Bell Labs no longer exists. China owns the entire solar supply chain — polysilicon processing, wafer production, module assembly, and the equipment to make all of it. The zero-to-one inventors lost to the one-to-a-hundred scalers so completely that the original invention is irrelevant. "What does it matter," Wang asks, "if you go from zero to one, if you cannot go from one to a hundred?"

DeepSeek, EVs, factory robotics, semiconductor manufacturing equipment — the pattern keeps repeating. Wang estimates roughly half of the serious people he spoke with on his book tour are still clinging to the zero-to-one distinction. He finds this increasingly hard to defend. "It starts sounding a little bit absurd. They can make all the toys and t-shirts, and they can make the large-capacity batteries for the EVs, but they can't..." The sentence doesn't need finishing.

Engineers vs. Lawyers: A Framework That Went Viral

The conceit that made Wang's book a Financial Times Best Book of 2025 — and that Adam Tooze was already using in London Review of Books keynotes without attribution — is the distinction between the engineering state and the lawyerly society. China is governed by engineers trained to execute. The United States is governed by lawyers trained to argue.

Wang is careful to note the idea wasn't original to him. Bill Clinton quipped about it in 1996 standing in China. But what Wang did was weaponize it — turn it into a framework sharp enough to actually explain how China's government operates and why, and how America's dysfunction looks from the outside. His preparation was almost absurdly perfect: years in Beijing, Hong Kong, and Shanghai analyzing Chinese semiconductor and manufacturing companies for Gavekal Research under Arthur Kroeber; then living through zero-COVID, a policy whose ambition was right there in the name; then moving immediately to a fellowship at Yale Law School, embedded among the people who would staff the Biden administration. Jake Sullivan. Gina Raimondo. Brian Dees. All Yale Law.

The contrast was visceral. Zero-COVID had the mathematical clarity of a missile engineer's optimization function — one of China's foundational zero-COVID architects was indeed a cybernetics expert. The lawyers at Yale operated differently. They were trained to make the best argument for whatever position they needed to occupy.

When host Steve Hsu pushes back — shouldn't scientists and engineers be frustrated with lawyers rather than deferential? — Wang flips the challenge. "Rather than decrying the lawyers for having seized all that power for themselves, I would say, why don't we point the finger at the economists, who had power tenuously, but then gave it up... Why don't we say to the engineers and the scientists, can you not get better at organizing society?" Power, as Nancy Pelosi once noted, is not given. It must be seized.

Wang is not naive about the engineering state's failures. Zero-COVID was a perfect expression of technocratic logic followed to a catastrophic conclusion — 25 million people locked in Shanghai because the math said zero. The One Child Policy was similarly the product of engineering-state thinking. Wang's prescription is pluralism: "What we want is pluralism. What we need are some scientists in the ruling elites, we need economists, lawyers, humanists." The problem with America is not the presence of lawyers. It is the near-total absence of everyone else.

The Industrial Party: An Online Meme That Became State Policy

Sometime in the early 2010s, before the Great Firewall had fully clamped down, a loose movement emerged on the Chinese internet. It had no party affiliation — there are only eight tolerated political parties in China, all subservient to the Communist Party, and this was not one of them. It was more of a meme, a current of thought advocating technocratic governance aimed at restoring Chinese scientific and industrial supremacy.

The Industrial Party, as it came to be known, produced canonical texts. One was called "Study or Wash Dishes." Another, "The Morning Star of Linggao," was online fan fiction imagining present-day people sent back to the Ming Dynasty to industrialize the island of Hainan. But Wang argues the deepest canonical text was Liu Cixin's The Three-Body Problem — humanity banding together under technocratic rule, building enormous navy-controlled spaceships to confront an existential extraterrestrial threat. That is the Industrial Party's political fantasy made literary.

Wang sees this worldview as deeply rooted in China's reading of its own history. For centuries, China understood itself as the preeminent civilization. Then, in a period it calls the Century of Humiliation, technologically superior barbarians — British, Dutch, eventually Japanese — arrived and inflicted extraordinary damage. The lesson absorbed: science and technology are what the barbarians used to beat us, and science and technology are what we must master to reclaim our place. This story, Wang notes, was told before the Communist Party and has been amplified by it into the bones of every Chinese person.

Wang offers a sharp counter-note: "What about the quarter century of self-humiliation that the Communist Party inflicted right afterwards?" The Great Leap Forward. The Cultural Revolution. The One Child Policy. The Strike Hard campaigns. These were not inflicted by barbarians. They emerged organically from within. The Industrial Party narrative conveniently omits this chapter.

The Ferocity No One Talks About

One of Wang's most striking claims, delivered almost as an aside: China embodies greater capitalist competition and greater capitalist excess than America does today. This from a country that still officially espouses Marxism.

American tech, Wang observes, is something like a gentleman's club. Google has search. Amazon has e-commerce. Facebook has social. They don't tread aggressively on each other's territory. Peter Thiel's famous dictum — competition is for losers, monopoly is the goal — is essentially the operating philosophy of Silicon Valley's biggest incumbents, and their political influence is sufficient to ensure the government mostly leaves them alone.

In China, everyone is fighting everyone over everything. Kyle Chan's chart of interlocking competitive relationships among Chinese tech companies looks like a circuit diagram from a nightmare. The result: profit margins driven toward zero, consumers with extraordinary buying power, and a Shanghai Composite index that has traded sideways for twenty years. Bad for Chinese equity investors. Spectacular for Chinese consumers. A wealthy American venture investor living in Beijing told Hsu he simply cannot replicate his quality of life in the United States, even as a billionaire. The food delivery, the services, the sheer purchasing power — none of it maps to what money buys in California.

The irony Wang highlights: China arrests Marxist labor organizers. There are no real unions. The workers have no protection from the relentless competitive pressure the party deliberately fosters. "What sort of socialism is this?"

Preparing Without Eagerness, Waging Without Preparing

Wang has a quote — one of his most cited — that he attributes to Viennese satirist Karl Kraus: "In Berlin, the situation is serious but not hopeless. Whereas in Vienna, the situation is hopeless but it is not serious." He applies this to the US-China cold war. Beijing has been preparing for Cold War without eagerness for waging it. Washington wants to wage a Cold War without preparing for it. America is Vienna.

China grew extraordinarily wealthy under the American-built liberal international order — becoming a major maritime trading power without building a large navy, which Wang notes may be historically unprecedented. Britain, Portugal, the Netherlands, the United States — all built navies to become trading powers. China got the trading power for free, underwritten by American security guarantees. That system is now turning against China. The Chinese are reacting, not initiating.

Meanwhile China has been quietly preparing for the siege: the Mayor's Vegetable Basket Program ensures farmland rings every major city for food self-sufficiency. Energy self-sufficiency is a stated national priority. Technological self-sufficiency — the semiconductor drive — was accelerated precisely by American export controls. Wang has been openly critical of those controls for years, writing in the New York Times and Foreign Affairs that they would ultimately stimulate Chinese self-sufficiency rather than prevent it. He appears to have been right.

The American failure, as Wang diagnoses it, is an unwillingness to pay the high costs required to actually compete. Reshoring manufacturing, rebuilding industrial capacity, funding science — these require sustained generational commitments. "America is not a serious country in a lot of different ways," he says, not cruelly but clinically.

Authoritarianism and Science: A More Complicated Relationship

One of the cherished beliefs of the lawyerly society is that political freedom is a prerequisite for scientific innovation. Free speech enables the productive collision of ideas. Suppression stifles breakthroughs. Wang interrogates this and finds it considerably more complicated.

He cites Harvard historian of science Lauren Graham, who studied the Soviet system and found something counterintuitive: the two most catastrophic events for Soviet scientists were the Stalinist purges of the 1930s and the dissolution of the Soviet Union in the 1990s. Which was worse? The dissolution. When the Soviet Union fell, the money stopped. Labs closed. Scientists emigrated or grew potatoes to survive. During the Stalin purges, by contrast, scientists staggered out of gulags and produced Nobel Prize-winning work, because Stalin funded science lavishly. "When you throw a lot of money at science, you're probably going to get a lot of science."

Hsu corroborates this from direct experience: Soviet mathematicians and physicists who emigrated to the US after 1991 were uniformly nostalgic for the closed system. Secret cities adjacent to weapons programs, where scientists wanted for nothing — food, salaries, orchestras, chess — in exchange for avoiding topics like the Ukrainian wheat harvest. The constraint was narrow. Within it, complete intellectual freedom. Hsu found their seminars ran for hours, fighting to the bottom of every question.

Wang will not say authoritarianism is good for science. What he will say: money is good for science. The Trump administration is cutting scientific funding. University budgets are contracting. China is funding science aggressively. "I think they're going to get the science."

The deeper point is structural. A physicist obsessed with improving a language model's attention mechanism is not thinking about politics. He is thinking about the problem. He can do that in Beijing as readily as in Mountain View — and in Beijing, unlike at Stanford, he can likely get direct access to frontier model training because Chinese universities and companies are more tightly integrated than their American counterparts.

Automation, AI, and the Danger of Magical Thinking

There is a fantasy circulating in American policy circles: that AI and automation will close the gap with China's industrial machine without requiring the hard work of actually rebuilding industrial capacity. Wang is not buying it.

Chinese factories are, on average, ahead on automation. Chinese Tesla workers are more productive than California Tesla workers. China installs as many industrial robots per year as the rest of the world combined. And China has massive advantages in AI training data from its manufacturing operations. The idea that American AI investments — currently approaching 2% of GDP in data center and compute CapEx — will magically solve the robotics gap, the port automation gap, the assembly line gap, strikes Wang as a category error. "We have to be careful not to let automation, like superintelligence, become an excuse for magical thinking rather than doing the hard work of capacity."

On the longer-horizon AI question — recursive self-improvement, existential risk — Wang is neither dismissive nor apocalyptic. He believes the recursive inflection point is real but not proximate, probably five to ten years out. He has participated in war games at the Tate Modern, led the China team against Stanley McChrystal's US team, and watched as both sides, facing the recursive moment, negotiated slowdowns and mutual lab inspections. That outcome gave him some optimism — not because it solves the problem, but because it demonstrates the possibility of the narrative.

He notes that Dario Amodei and Demis Hassabis, at Davos, both said they are in communication and watching for the recursive inflection point together, and both expressed a preference for slower timelines. They would slow down if they could. The problem is collective action: it only takes one lab — one nation, one billionaire with a data center in the UAE — to defect from any slowdown agreement. "It's a little bit like global warming."

Wang ends with a point that loops back to the lawyers-versus-engineers frame. Scientists and engineers need to get better at narrative. The war game at the Tate Modern produced an optimistic ending precisely because narrative matters — because the ape brain responds to enacted story in a way it does not respond to technical arguments. "We are all ruled by ape brains. And so this is why the lawyers are in charge. And the scientists and engineers need to get better at narrative."

What To Do With This

  • Retire the zero-to-one / one-to-a-hundred framework when assessing Chinese competition. It is now empirically indefensible and serves mainly to produce complacency in Western strategy and investment.
  • Track where elite Chinese AI researchers are choosing to work, and ask why. The 90% retention figure in the US is real but may not hold; the Beijing academic-industry integration is a genuine structural advantage that could shift talent flows.
  • Study the Industrial Party's canonical texts — especially The Three-Body Problem as political philosophy, not just science fiction — to understand the motivating worldview of Chinese technocrats and the engineers who build for them.
  • Assess any American AI or automation investment against the specific industrial gaps China leads in (port automation, factory robotics, EV supply chain) rather than assuming general AI progress closes those gaps automatically.
  • Evaluate US semiconductor export controls against their actual effect: Wang's long-standing argument — that they stimulate Chinese self-sufficiency rather than preventing it — is tracking as correct, and strategy should update accordingly.
  • When evaluating scientific productivity claims about authoritarian systems, ask whether the constraint is narrow or totalizing. Soviet scientists worked well inside a narrow constraint; the analogy to China is not perfect but is closer than Western commentators typically acknowledge.
  • Take Chinese consumer market purchasing power seriously as a talent retention tool. Quality of life for high earners in Chinese cities, driven by ferocious service competition, is a real factor in decisions about where to live and work.
  • Do not treat AI as a substitute for industrial policy. Capacity building in manufacturing, logistics, and physical automation requires sustained multi-decade investment regardless of what language models can do. Wang's warning against "magical thinking" is the most actionable policy insight in the conversation.
  • Build or support channels for AI lab coordination across geopolitical lines now, before the recursive inflection point. The collective action problem becomes much harder to solve at speed; the infrastructure for cooperation should be established in advance.

Lines Worth Keeping

"I want to suggest, no, let's bury this idea too, that the Chinese are able to both innovate as well as scale, that numbers are continuous. This idea of going from zero to one as just exclusively the remit of the West — I think is not empirical at this point."

— Dan Wang
"What does it matter if you go from zero to one, if you cannot go from one to a hundred?"

— Dan Wang
"Probably the most underrated part of the Chinese system is the ferocity of market competition. It's excusable not to see that, given that the party espouses so much Marxism. I would argue that China embodies both greater capitalist competition and greater capitalist excess than America does today."

— Dan Wang
"Beijing has been preparing for Cold War without eagerness for waging it. The US wants to wage a Cold War without preparing for it."

— Dan Wang
"Rather than decrying the lawyers for having seized all that power for themselves, why don't we say to the engineers and the scientists: can you not get better at organizing society, can you not make a case better to the population, and try to get that power away from them?"

— Dan Wang

item.title: The Word That Reveals Everything Wrong With AI Customer Service
item.description:
The Word That Reveals Everything Wrong With AI Customer Service

Matt Price has a problem with the word "containment." After thirteen years at Zendesk watching customer service software evolve—and now as CEO of Crescendo AI, a company with over 500 deployments—he uses that single word to expose the rotted foundation beneath most AI customer service implementations.

"There is a term in CX called deflection and containment," Price says. "Most bot companies are talking about containment, as if consumers are some kind of virus that you can't let near your organization." The language isn't accidental. It reflects a philosophy—that customers are a cost to be minimized, a problem to be routed away from humans, a liability to be "contained." And Price believes that philosophy, now being encoded into the first generation of AI bots, will prove fatal to the companies that embrace it.

His bet is the opposite: that the companies that survive and thrive over the next five years will be the ones that treat customer service not as overhead but as a competitive frontier—and that AI, properly architected, is the enabler of that transformation rather than a replacement for it.

Why Bolting AI onto Broken Processes Fails

Price joined Crescendo in 2024 after leaving Zendesk Labs, arriving at a moment when LLMs had just made something genuinely new possible. His framing of that moment is stark: the introduction of large language models represents a shift in customer care "bigger than anything since maybe the introduction of the telephone." Not a marginal improvement—a category change.

But the first wave of adoption has largely wasted that opportunity. The dominant approach has been what Price calls "bolt-on": companies take their existing customer service processes, preserve their structure, and attach an AI layer on top. CSAT scores drop. Labor savings don't materialize because the AI and the human workforce aren't integrated—the BPO vendor wants to sell more people-hours, and the bot vendor wants to maximize AI resolutions, and neither is aligned with the actual customer outcome.

"BPO people, all they want to do is provide more people. Technology people, bot vendors, all they want to do is maximize the amount of work the AI is doing," Price explains. The result is that companies invest in AI bots without seeing representative savings in their labor costs, and customers experience the friction of handoffs between systems that weren't designed to work together.

Crescendo's response was structurally radical: they are simultaneously a technology company and a contact center operation. They acquired a large BPO business—thousands of people—because they wanted to understand, from the inside, exactly what it would take to optimize the full process. "We wanted to know from the inside out exactly how to optimize the product and process and conversion of these businesses to AI." This is not a software company selling a platform. It's a company that has eaten the whole problem.

The Architecture of an AI-Native Contact Center

Crescendo's technical approach reflects the same end-to-end thinking. Price distinguishes between "first-generation" LLM bots—heavily scripted, workflow-driven, where chunks of instructions are sequenced and sent to a language model and responses collected—and what he considers the next generation: autonomous, multimodal agents that can reason about which tools to use without being explicitly told.

The clearest illustration is a customer they call RASHO, a smart lawn irrigation company. A customer sets up the device through an app. The AI agent can check device connectivity via an MCP connection, diagnose what's wrong, switch from text to voice if the customer needs their hands free, ask for a photo of the device to scan, and talk them through a physical reset—all within a single interaction, all from the same interface, sequencing modalities as the situation demands.

"What we've done is we've elevated things up a level further and given more autonomy to the AI. We don't have to give it any more instructions. It's smart enough now to know, well, if I'm asking them to check the device, maybe I should check it myself first. Or smart enough to know that if I'm asking somebody to do something with their hands, maybe I should flip it to a voice interaction."

This matters technically because the bolt-on approach forces companies to rebuild everything for each channel. Build a chat workflow, then rebuild it for voice, then rebuild it for email—because the underlying architecture sequences instructions per modality. Crescendo's agentic architecture sits above that layer, which means a single configuration can deploy across channels and adapt in real time.

Reliability is baked in at the infrastructure level. If OpenAI goes down, customer service can't go down with it. Crescendo operates a multi-LLM orchestration layer that can fail over between models—OpenAI, Claude, Gemini—dynamically. This also gives them a competitive advantage as base models improve: they can route to better-performing models as capabilities evolve, without rebuilding anything. Clients, Price notes, generally don't care which model is running—they care about outcomes, and they trust Crescendo to make the model selection.

Accuracy across the system runs at about 99.5 percent. Standalone AI typically operates in the low-to-mid 90s; human agents are also typically in the 90s. The gap is achieved through a combination of curated knowledge (Crescendo helps clients clean and organize their knowledge base before deployment—bad training data is the primary source of inaccuracies, not hallucinations per se), continuous calibration, and human-in-the-loop scoring. Every interaction is analyzed by AI across multiple vectors: customer sentiment, whether the problem was resolved, compliance with regulations, sales intent. Interactions that fall below quality thresholds aren't billed.

The Business Model as a Forcing Function

The pricing model isn't ancillary to the product—it's load-bearing to the entire philosophy. Crescendo charges per successful outcome. Not per conversation, not per interaction, but per solved problem. And critically: they don't charge at all if the customer experience was negative.

"Whether it's handled by the AI or the human, we only get paid when we solve that outcome, and we never charge a customer if it was a negative customer experience, because we can do that now."

This is only possible because the AI tooling now exists to actually assess outcome quality at scale. For years, the theoretical ROI of customer service software never materialized in practice—companies bought seats, invested implementation time, and didn't see the results. Outcome-based pricing shifts the risk to Crescendo and aligns every incentive in the system toward actual resolution. If the AI doesn't outperform the client's existing system, Crescendo doesn't charge until it does.

Price is clear that this is a significant driver of their growth rate. It's also a structural moat: the ability to make this guarantee depends on having both the AI capability and the human backstop—neither alone is sufficient to be confident enough in outcomes to stake revenue on them.

Where Humans Actually Belong

The human-in-the-loop model at Crescendo is not the one most people picture when they hear "AI plus humans." It's not a fallback queue where overflow tickets go when the bot fails. It's a set of specialized roles that have been redesigned around what AI genuinely cannot do.

When the AI reaches its "acceptable boundaries"—when a problem requires judgment, expertise, or authorization it doesn't have—the escalation goes not to the back of an email queue, but to a trained specialist who is ready and equipped. For a medical company client, that means HIPAA-certified representatives. For a financial services firm, it means compliance-trained agents. The specialization is procured and maintained by Crescendo as part of the service.

The more interesting transformation happens in the non-escalation roles. Price describes a QA manager whose traditional job involved sampling calls, scoring them against a rubric, and filing reports. That work is now almost entirely automated. What that person does instead: they review AI-generated insights across all interactions proactively, and surface business intelligence that no human workforce could have produced at scale. "As a shoe manufacturer, you've got a sizing issue on this line and you're generating these refunds. We recommend that you put a sizing guide on your website." The role has shifted from functional compliance work to strategic advising.

Crescendo describes these people as "superhumans"—workers whose capabilities have been amplified by AI such that they can operate at a level of analysis and insight that wasn't achievable in a labor-intensive environment. Six distinct roles have been redesigned in this way. Turnover has dropped significantly. Price's explanation is simple: repetitive work isn't fulfilling, and these roles are no longer repetitive.

Customer Service as Product Innovation

The most ambitious claim Price makes—the one that contextualizes everything else—is that customer service innovation is becoming as strategically important as product innovation. He grounds this in an example from a beauty company client. Their biggest driver of returns was color mismatch: customers bought cosmetics that didn't match their skin tone. Crescendo implemented AI-powered color matching through their customer service interface. The downstream effect isn't just fewer returns—it's a reduced need for physical retail presence, because the service that used to require a trip to Ulta can now happen through a customer's phone.

RASHO, the irrigation company, is moving from reactive to proactive support: when a customer buys the product, they're immediately connected to an AI that walks them through setup. The customer service interaction becomes part of the product onboarding, eliminating the moment where customers consult a paper manual or search YouTube.

LovePop, a pop-up greeting card company with a generous returns policy, had a staffing problem during peak seasons: by the time a human got back to a customer with a return or replacement offer, the emotional moment had passed. The customer needed the card for their mom's birthday now. AI-first response changed the economics of that exchange—the offer arrives in seconds, not eight hours. TrustPilot rating: from 3.6 to 4.6 in a few months.

Price quotes Ken Chenault, former Chairman of American Express, on the emotional architecture of brand: "Brands are a connection of two things. There's the logical, which is the service the product offers. And then there's the emotional connection. And there's no better place where the emotional gets activated to a brand is within customer care, because that's where the human interaction actually takes place." The corollary he draws is counterintuitive: as AI handles more volume, the moments when a human does engage become more meaningful signals of investment. "If I am allowed to speak to a human, then I know that that's a costly resource for you, and you're really going out of your way to make that connection with me."

The Consolidation Play

Crescendo is actively pursuing acquisition of founder-led BPO businesses. Price's logic: most independent contact center operators have tried to enable AI, hit the limits of their technical depth, and wound up as third-party customers of bot vendors—still in the bolt-on trap. The major BPO players are already showing signs of contraction; TTEC attempted a go-private transaction that failed amid declining share prices, and growth is slowing across the category.

For founders who want to give their clients and employees a future in an AI-native world, Crescendo offers a path: bring your talent into the forward-deployed model, upgrade your clients to the AI-native platform, and benefit from the depth of AI capability that an independent services company couldn't realistically attract or retain.

Price is explicit about not wanting quick exits. He's building a category—"AI-native customer concierge companies"—and expects Crescendo to be its defining example. The five-year vision: "People will look back and say, why did we ever try and do that ourselves? It's a bit like in anybody that's a mid-market manufacturer now, why would we ever set up a production line and tool it ourselves and staff it ourselves when we can design a product and work with a contract manufacturer to deliver it for us?"

What This Means for People in the Industry

Price is asked directly what skills someone currently working in customer service should develop to stay relevant. His answer is practical and doesn't lean on reassurance. First: AI literacy is now foundational—learn to prompt, learn to improve your prompts with AI, stay current on capabilities. It's table stakes, like learning to type was once table stakes.

Second, and more important: the skills that made great customer service people great before AI—empathy, careful listening, diligence in follow-up—are exactly the skills that AI cannot replicate and that become more valuable as AI handles more volume. The people who were copying and pasting macros into chat windows were always in a precarious position; AI simply makes that visible. The people who were genuinely good at human connection have been underutilized by an industry that structured their jobs around repetitive throughput.

AI also acts, in Price's framing, as a democratizer. A brilliant electronics technician who doesn't speak fluent English has been limited in who they can serve. AI removes that barrier—translating, smoothing communication—and allows expertise to reach anyone who needs it. That's not a threat to the expert; it's an expansion of their reach.

The industry's 40% annual turnover rate wasn't natural law. It was a consequence of designing jobs that were repetitive, seasonal, and fundamentally not fulfilling. Crescendo's attrition is substantially lower—a direct result of the job redesign that happens when AI takes over the repeatable parts and humans are redeployed to the work that requires judgment.

The CX leader who spent decades budget-starved and relegated to firefighting—called into the boardroom only when a complaint reached the CEO—is the figure Price most wants to rehabilitate. "This is a new era where CX leaders get back in the boardroom and they get to be innovators." The head of customer support at RASHO has gone from being a problem-solver and people-manager to being a customer experience creator. That's not a consolation prize. That's the actual job.

What To Do With This

  • Audit your AI deployment for "containment" thinking. If your current bot is designed to deflect and prevent customers from reaching humans, the architecture is misaligned with customer outcomes. Map what happens when the bot fails—if it routes to a generic email queue, that's the problem to fix first.
  • Redesign human escalation before deploying AI, not after. The most common mistake is dropping AI in and pushing humans back to the old experience. Define specifically who handles escalations, what expertise they have, and how they receive context from the AI before you go live.
  • Switch to outcome-based evaluation of your AI vendor. If you're paying per conversation or per seat, your vendor's incentives aren't aligned with resolution quality. Push for per-successful-outcome pricing, or at minimum build outcome-quality tracking into your contract terms.
  • Map your AI training data for accuracy, not just coverage. Most "hallucinations" are actually inaccuracies from bad source material—outdated articles, inconsistent agent responses, wrong information on your website. Audit your knowledge base before assuming the model is the problem.
  • Build multi-LLM failover into any production deployment. A single-provider AI system has availability risk. Architect for the ability to route between models (OpenAI, Claude, Gemini) so that provider outages don't take down customer-facing systems.
  • Identify which QA roles in your organization can be shifted from sampling to insights. If a QA manager is manually reviewing a 5% sample of calls, AI can score 100% of interactions. Redeploy that person to proactively surface product and process issues from the full data, not a sample.
  • Move at least one customer service touchpoint from reactive to proactive. The RASHO model—connecting customers to AI-assisted setup immediately after purchase—eliminates a support ticket before it's filed. Identify the highest-volume reactive support category and design a proactive intervention for it.
  • Retrain your customer service team on AI literacy before deploying AI to them. Price's experience is that when agents understand what AI can and can't do, their confidence and engagement go up. Prompting skills and AI workflow familiarity aren't optional extras—they're the foundation for the new role.
  • Evaluate multimodal capability before choosing a platform. Single-channel bots (text only, or voice only) force you to rebuild flows for every new channel. A multimodal architecture that can switch between text, voice, image, and device integration within a single session reduces long-term rebuild costs and handles more complex support scenarios.
  • Consider the emotional signal value of human availability as a feature, not just a cost. As AI handles volume, the choice to make a human available becomes a differentiator. Design your escalation paths to make the human moment feel like an upgrade, not a failure state—because customers will notice.

Lines Worth Keeping

"Most bot companies are talking about containment, as if consumers are some kind of virus that you can't let near your organization."

— Matt Price

"Whether it's handled by the AI or the human, we only get paid when we solve that outcome, and we never charge a customer if it was a negative customer experience, because we can do that now."

— Matt Price

"If I am allowed to speak to a human, then I know that that's a costly resource for you, and you're really going out of your way to make that connection with me."

— Matt Price

"Innovation in customer care is going to become as important as innovation in product."

— Matt Price

"To make AI work in automation, we're being incredibly flexible about where you inject people, and then continually evolving that is a valuable service."

— Matt Price

"For 10, 20, 30 years, they've been downtrodden, budget starved. The only time the board and leadership once talked to them about is when they received a direct complaint as to what's going to happen. AI changes that. This is a new era where CX leaders get back in the boardroom and they get to be innovators."

— Matt Price

"LLMs really create a world of abundance in CX. The first time things are not constrained and businesses can open up the door to the interaction."

— Matt Price
item.content:encoded:
The Word That Reveals Everything Wrong With AI Customer Service

Matt Price has a problem with the word "containment." After thirteen years at Zendesk watching customer service software evolve—and now as CEO of Crescendo AI, a company with over 500 deployments—he uses that single word to expose the rotted foundation beneath most AI customer service implementations.

"There is a term in CX called deflection and containment," Price says. "Most bot companies are talking about containment, as if consumers are some kind of virus that you can't let near your organization." The language isn't accidental. It reflects a philosophy—that customers are a cost to be minimized, a problem to be routed away from humans, a liability to be "contained." And Price believes that philosophy, now being encoded into the first generation of AI bots, will prove fatal to the companies that embrace it.

His bet is the opposite: that the companies that survive and thrive over the next five years will be the ones that treat customer service not as overhead but as a competitive frontier—and that AI, properly architected, is the enabler of that transformation rather than a replacement for it.

Why Bolting AI onto Broken Processes Fails

Price joined Crescendo in 2024 after leaving Zendesk Labs, arriving at a moment when LLMs had just made something genuinely new possible. His framing of that moment is stark: the introduction of large language models represents a shift in customer care "bigger than anything since maybe the introduction of the telephone." Not a marginal improvement—a category change.

But the first wave of adoption has largely wasted that opportunity. The dominant approach has been what Price calls "bolt-on": companies take their existing customer service processes, preserve their structure, and attach an AI layer on top. CSAT scores drop. Labor savings don't materialize because the AI and the human workforce aren't integrated—the BPO vendor wants to sell more people-hours, and the bot vendor wants to maximize AI resolutions, and neither is aligned with the actual customer outcome.

"BPO people, all they want to do is provide more people. Technology people, bot vendors, all they want to do is maximize the amount of work the AI is doing," Price explains. The result is that companies invest in AI bots without seeing representative savings in their labor costs, and customers experience the friction of handoffs between systems that weren't designed to work together.

Crescendo's response was structurally radical: they are simultaneously a technology company and a contact center operation. They acquired a large BPO business—thousands of people—because they wanted to understand, from the inside, exactly what it would take to optimize the full process. "We wanted to know from the inside out exactly how to optimize the product and process and conversion of these businesses to AI." This is not a software company selling a platform. It's a company that has eaten the whole problem.

The Architecture of an AI-Native Contact Center

Crescendo's technical approach reflects the same end-to-end thinking. Price distinguishes between "first-generation" LLM bots—heavily scripted, workflow-driven, where chunks of instructions are sequenced and sent to a language model and responses collected—and what he considers the next generation: autonomous, multimodal agents that can reason about which tools to use without being explicitly told.

The clearest illustration is a customer they call RASHO, a smart lawn irrigation company. A customer sets up the device through an app. The AI agent can check device connectivity via an MCP connection, diagnose what's wrong, switch from text to voice if the customer needs their hands free, ask for a photo of the device to scan, and talk them through a physical reset—all within a single interaction, all from the same interface, sequencing modalities as the situation demands.

"What we've done is we've elevated things up a level further and given more autonomy to the AI. We don't have to give it any more instructions. It's smart enough now to know, well, if I'm asking them to check the device, maybe I should check it myself first. Or smart enough to know that if I'm asking somebody to do something with their hands, maybe I should flip it to a voice interaction."

This matters technically because the bolt-on approach forces companies to rebuild everything for each channel. Build a chat workflow, then rebuild it for voice, then rebuild it for email—because the underlying architecture sequences instructions per modality. Crescendo's agentic architecture sits above that layer, which means a single configuration can deploy across channels and adapt in real time.

Reliability is baked in at the infrastructure level. If OpenAI goes down, customer service can't go down with it. Crescendo operates a multi-LLM orchestration layer that can fail over between models—OpenAI, Claude, Gemini—dynamically. This also gives them a competitive advantage as base models improve: they can route to better-performing models as capabilities evolve, without rebuilding anything. Clients, Price notes, generally don't care which model is running—they care about outcomes, and they trust Crescendo to make the model selection.

Accuracy across the system runs at about 99.5 percent. Standalone AI typically operates in the low-to-mid 90s; human agents are also typically in the 90s. The gap is achieved through a combination of curated knowledge (Crescendo helps clients clean and organize their knowledge base before deployment—bad training data is the primary source of inaccuracies, not hallucinations per se), continuous calibration, and human-in-the-loop scoring. Every interaction is analyzed by AI across multiple vectors: customer sentiment, whether the problem was resolved, compliance with regulations, sales intent. Interactions that fall below quality thresholds aren't billed.

The Business Model as a Forcing Function

The pricing model isn't ancillary to the product—it's load-bearing to the entire philosophy. Crescendo charges per successful outcome. Not per conversation, not per interaction, but per solved problem. And critically: they don't charge at all if the customer experience was negative.

"Whether it's handled by the AI or the human, we only get paid when we solve that outcome, and we never charge a customer if it was a negative customer experience, because we can do that now."

This is only possible because the AI tooling now exists to actually assess outcome quality at scale. For years, the theoretical ROI of customer service software never materialized in practice—companies bought seats, invested implementation time, and didn't see the results. Outcome-based pricing shifts the risk to Crescendo and aligns every incentive in the system toward actual resolution. If the AI doesn't outperform the client's existing system, Crescendo doesn't charge until it does.

Price is clear that this is a significant driver of their growth rate. It's also a structural moat: the ability to make this guarantee depends on having both the AI capability and the human backstop—neither alone is sufficient to be confident enough in outcomes to stake revenue on them.

Where Humans Actually Belong

The human-in-the-loop model at Crescendo is not the one most people picture when they hear "AI plus humans." It's not a fallback queue where overflow tickets go when the bot fails. It's a set of specialized roles that have been redesigned around what AI genuinely cannot do.

When the AI reaches its "acceptable boundaries"—when a problem requires judgment, expertise, or authorization it doesn't have—the escalation goes not to the back of an email queue, but to a trained specialist who is ready and equipped. For a medical company client, that means HIPAA-certified representatives. For a financial services firm, it means compliance-trained agents. The specialization is procured and maintained by Crescendo as part of the service.

The more interesting transformation happens in the non-escalation roles. Price describes a QA manager whose traditional job involved sampling calls, scoring them against a rubric, and filing reports. That work is now almost entirely automated. What that person does instead: they review AI-generated insights across all interactions proactively, and surface business intelligence that no human workforce could have produced at scale. "As a shoe manufacturer, you've got a sizing issue on this line and you're generating these refunds. We recommend that you put a sizing guide on your website." The role has shifted from functional compliance work to strategic advising.

Crescendo describes these people as "superhumans"—workers whose capabilities have been amplified by AI such that they can operate at a level of analysis and insight that wasn't achievable in a labor-intensive environment. Six distinct roles have been redesigned in this way. Turnover has dropped significantly. Price's explanation is simple: repetitive work isn't fulfilling, and these roles are no longer repetitive.

Customer Service as Product Innovation

The most ambitious claim Price makes—the one that contextualizes everything else—is that customer service innovation is becoming as strategically important as product innovation. He grounds this in an example from a beauty company client. Their biggest driver of returns was color mismatch: customers bought cosmetics that didn't match their skin tone. Crescendo implemented AI-powered color matching through their customer service interface. The downstream effect isn't just fewer returns—it's a reduced need for physical retail presence, because the service that used to require a trip to Ulta can now happen through a customer's phone.

RASHO, the irrigation company, is moving from reactive to proactive support: when a customer buys the product, they're immediately connected to an AI that walks them through setup. The customer service interaction becomes part of the product onboarding, eliminating the moment where customers consult a paper manual or search YouTube.

LovePop, a pop-up greeting card company with a generous returns policy, had a staffing problem during peak seasons: by the time a human got back to a customer with a return or replacement offer, the emotional moment had passed. The customer needed the card for their mom's birthday now. AI-first response changed the economics of that exchange—the offer arrives in seconds, not eight hours. TrustPilot rating: from 3.6 to 4.6 in a few months.

Price quotes Ken Chenault, former Chairman of American Express, on the emotional architecture of brand: "Brands are a connection of two things. There's the logical, which is the service the product offers. And then there's the emotional connection. And there's no better place where the emotional gets activated to a brand is within customer care, because that's where the human interaction actually takes place." The corollary he draws is counterintuitive: as AI handles more volume, the moments when a human does engage become more meaningful signals of investment. "If I am allowed to speak to a human, then I know that that's a costly resource for you, and you're really going out of your way to make that connection with me."

The Consolidation Play

Crescendo is actively pursuing acquisition of founder-led BPO businesses. Price's logic: most independent contact center operators have tried to enable AI, hit the limits of their technical depth, and wound up as third-party customers of bot vendors—still in the bolt-on trap. The major BPO players are already showing signs of contraction; TTEC attempted a go-private transaction that failed amid declining share prices, and growth is slowing across the category.

For founders who want to give their clients and employees a future in an AI-native world, Crescendo offers a path: bring your talent into the forward-deployed model, upgrade your clients to the AI-native platform, and benefit from the depth of AI capability that an independent services company couldn't realistically attract or retain.

Price is explicit about not wanting quick exits. He's building a category—"AI-native customer concierge companies"—and expects Crescendo to be its defining example. The five-year vision: "People will look back and say, why did we ever try and do that ourselves? It's a bit like in anybody that's a mid-market manufacturer now, why would we ever set up a production line and tool it ourselves and staff it ourselves when we can design a product and work with a contract manufacturer to deliver it for us?"

What This Means for People in the Industry

Price is asked directly what skills someone currently working in customer service should develop to stay relevant. His answer is practical and doesn't lean on reassurance. First: AI literacy is now foundational—learn to prompt, learn to improve your prompts with AI, stay current on capabilities. It's table stakes, like learning to type was once table stakes.

Second, and more important: the skills that made great customer service people great before AI—empathy, careful listening, diligence in follow-up—are exactly the skills that AI cannot replicate and that become more valuable as AI handles more volume. The people who were copying and pasting macros into chat windows were always in a precarious position; AI simply makes that visible. The people who were genuinely good at human connection have been underutilized by an industry that structured their jobs around repetitive throughput.

AI also acts, in Price's framing, as a democratizer. A brilliant electronics technician who doesn't speak fluent English has been limited in who they can serve. AI removes that barrier—translating, smoothing communication—and allows expertise to reach anyone who needs it. That's not a threat to the expert; it's an expansion of their reach.

The industry's 40% annual turnover rate wasn't natural law. It was a consequence of designing jobs that were repetitive, seasonal, and fundamentally not fulfilling. Crescendo's attrition is substantially lower—a direct result of the job redesign that happens when AI takes over the repeatable parts and humans are redeployed to the work that requires judgment.

The CX leader who spent decades budget-starved and relegated to firefighting—called into the boardroom only when a complaint reached the CEO—is the figure Price most wants to rehabilitate. "This is a new era where CX leaders get back in the boardroom and they get to be innovators." The head of customer support at RASHO has gone from being a problem-solver and people-manager to being a customer experience creator. That's not a consolation prize. That's the actual job.

What To Do With This

  • Audit your AI deployment for "containment" thinking. If your current bot is designed to deflect and prevent customers from reaching humans, the architecture is misaligned with customer outcomes. Map what happens when the bot fails—if it routes to a generic email queue, that's the problem to fix first.
  • Redesign human escalation before deploying AI, not after. The most common mistake is dropping AI in and pushing humans back to the old experience. Define specifically who handles escalations, what expertise they have, and how they receive context from the AI before you go live.
  • Switch to outcome-based evaluation of your AI vendor. If you're paying per conversation or per seat, your vendor's incentives aren't aligned with resolution quality. Push for per-successful-outcome pricing, or at minimum build outcome-quality tracking into your contract terms.
  • Map your AI training data for accuracy, not just coverage. Most "hallucinations" are actually inaccuracies from bad source material—outdated articles, inconsistent agent responses, wrong information on your website. Audit your knowledge base before assuming the model is the problem.
  • Build multi-LLM failover into any production deployment. A single-provider AI system has availability risk. Architect for the ability to route between models (OpenAI, Claude, Gemini) so that provider outages don't take down customer-facing systems.
  • Identify which QA roles in your organization can be shifted from sampling to insights. If a QA manager is manually reviewing a 5% sample of calls, AI can score 100% of interactions. Redeploy that person to proactively surface product and process issues from the full data, not a sample.
  • Move at least one customer service touchpoint from reactive to proactive. The RASHO model—connecting customers to AI-assisted setup immediately after purchase—eliminates a support ticket before it's filed. Identify the highest-volume reactive support category and design a proactive intervention for it.
  • Retrain your customer service team on AI literacy before deploying AI to them. Price's experience is that when agents understand what AI can and can't do, their confidence and engagement go up. Prompting skills and AI workflow familiarity aren't optional extras—they're the foundation for the new role.
  • Evaluate multimodal capability before choosing a platform. Single-channel bots (text only, or voice only) force you to rebuild flows for every new channel. A multimodal architecture that can switch between text, voice, image, and device integration within a single session reduces long-term rebuild costs and handles more complex support scenarios.
  • Consider the emotional signal value of human availability as a feature, not just a cost. As AI handles volume, the choice to make a human available becomes a differentiator. Design your escalation paths to make the human moment feel like an upgrade, not a failure state—because customers will notice.

"Most bot companies are talking about containment, as if consumers are some kind of virus that you can't let near your organization."

— Matt Price

"Whether it's handled by the AI or the human, we only get paid when we solve that outcome, and we never charge a customer if it was a negative customer experience, because we can do that now."

— Matt Price

"Innovation in customer care is going to become as important as innovation in product."

— Matt Price
