"There are some teams where their EM is managing quite a few people and they're doing it pretty adeptly because of some of these tools," Wu says.

The 100% Codex Codebase Experiment

Inside OpenAI, one team is running an experiment with no escape hatch. They maintain a codebase written entirely by Codex. No switching to tab-completion or Cursor when the agent gets stuck. No rolling up your sleeves to fix it manually. If the agent cannot do it, the problem is to figure out why the agent cannot do it.

The learnings are substantial. When Codex fails to do what you want, the root cause is almost always context. "It's either under-specified or there's just not enough information around how to do something available to the agent," Wu says. The solution is not to abandon the agent but to encode more tribal knowledge into the repository itself — through code comments, code structure, markdown files, skills files, anything that gives the model better information to work with.

This reframes the problem entirely. The bottleneck is not the model's capability. The bottleneck is the information environment the model operates in. Engineers who understand this stop asking "why won't the agent do this?" and start asking "what does the agent need to know to do this?"

Wu describes the other psychological dimension of this experiment: the stress of agents not working as expected. "I've been hearing more and more there's this stress people feel when their agents aren't working," he says. Engineers who have always had the escape hatch of just writing the code themselves are now confronting what it means to work exclusively through delegation. It is a different cognitive mode. And it is, Wu believes, where the entire profession is heading.

The Models Will Eat Your Scaffolding for Breakfast

One of Wu's most important observations about the AI industry is also one of its most uncomfortable: listening too closely to customers is not always the right strategy. In a normal industry, this would be heresy. In AI, it is almost a survival principle.

"The field and the models themselves are just changing so, so quickly," Wu says. "They tend to disrupt themselves."

The phrase he keeps returning to comes from Nicolas, founder of FinTool: the models will eat your scaffolding for breakfast. In 2022, when ChatGPT launched, the models were raw. The developer ecosystem responded by building elaborate scaffolding — agent frameworks, vector stores, complex orchestration layers — to compensate for model limitations. Those limitations then disappeared, and much of the scaffolding became unnecessary.

"If you rewind back to 2022, there was all this product scaffolding to basically try and steer the model and build a framework around it to get it to do what you want," Wu says. "As you've seen the field play out, the models have just changed so much and gotten so much better that they ended up literally eating some of the scaffolding."

Vector stores are the clearest example. In 2023, vectorizing an entire corpus and optimizing retrieval felt like the essential pattern for bringing organizational context into models. Today, the better approach is to give the model search tools and let it reason — a much simpler architecture that produces better results. The entire ecosystem that had formed around the old approach became, in Wu's word, a local maximum.

The implication for builders is significant. If you listen only to what customers say they want right now — a better vector store, a more robust agent framework — you will build something excellent for where the models are today. You will not build something excellent for where the models are going. And because the models are moving very quickly, "today" is a dangerous place to optimize for.

"My general advice, and I've been giving this to people for a while and I think it's still true today," Wu says, "is make sure you're building for where the models are going and not where they are today." He has watched startups build products for capabilities the models could do at 80% — products that worked but felt almost-there. Then the models improved, and those products became exceptional. Building ahead of the curve requires accepting some friction and some wait time. Given the pace of model improvement, the wait is often short.

Wu is candid that OpenAI's own API team has made this mistake. "We kind of took some left and right turns when we shouldn't have," he says. "But yeah, the models still end up — models get better, and we're all learning the bitter lesson day in and day out." The reference is to the famous "bitter lesson" from AI research: adding human-designed scaffolding and logic tends to lose in the long run to systems that simply let models scale and learn on their own.

The Second and Third Order Effects Nobody Is Pricing In

The idea of the one-person billion-dollar startup has become a kind of ambient meme in AI circles — a vision of what extreme individual leverage looks like at the limit. Wu finds the concept compelling, but he is more interested in what it implies for the economy around it.

"I think people aren't really pricing in the second or third order effects of this," he says.

If one person can run a billion-dollar company, it also means it is far easier to run companies of any size. Wu's prediction: a massive explosion in startups, particularly vertical B2B SaaS. "To enable a one-person billion-dollar startup, there might be 100 other small startups building bespoke software that works extremely well to support other types of small one-person billion-dollar startups." The one-person newsletter or podcast that might become a large business does not need to solve support infrastructure internally. A small startup will build software specifically for that problem, priced for a small team, written by AI in a fraction of the time it once would have taken.

"There might be like 100 million-dollar startups. There might be tens of thousands of 10-million-dollar startups," Wu says. "And as an individual, it's actually pretty great to have a 10-million-dollar business. That's like enough for yourself for life at that point."

The further-order effects are harder to predict but worth thinking about. Wu sketches a world where VC economics shift: if more value accrues to 10- to 50-million-dollar companies rather than the venture-scale giants that deliver 100x returns, the structure of the funding ecosystem changes with it. The high-agency individual who builds a highly profitable micro-company is well-served by this world. The fund manager seeking billion-dollar outcomes has a harder time.

The deeper point is about distribution. In a world full of startups, each fighting for attention, having an existing audience and platform becomes an extraordinary moat. "Distribution becomes increasingly important because there are so many things trying to get your attention," Wu observes.

The Negative ROI Deployment Problem

Wu has a vantage point that almost no one else does: he talks to essentially every AI company and enterprise deploying AI at scale. His observation about a large fraction of AI deployments is bracing. "I would not be surprised if a lot of AI deployments are actually negative ROI," he says.

The diagnosis is not about model capability. It is about adoption dynamics. "We in Silicon Valley just forget that we live in a bubble," Wu says. "We are so — Twitter is a bubble, X is a bubble, Silicon Valley is a bubble, software engineering is a bubble. Most people in the world are not software engineers, are not very AI-pilled, are not following every single model release."

When Wu talks to employees at companies deploying AI outside of tech, he finds something striking: "It's like the most basic thing that they're trying to do. They have very little understanding of exactly how this technology works. They're asking very simple questions of these things. They're really not pushing it just yet."

The failure mode he sees most often is deployment that is purely top-down. C-suite buys the tools, issues the mandate, adds AI usage to performance reviews. Employees are told to use the tools but have no community of practice around them, no examples, no one to learn from. "They're like, I know I'm supposed to use this, and maybe it's on my performance review too, but I'm not sure what to do," Wu says. "They look around, no one else is doing it, there's no one else to learn from."

His recommendation is specific: find the people who are most genuinely excited about AI — often technical-adjacent roles, not software engineers, since most companies don't have software engineers — and build them into an internal tiger team. Let them explore the full range of capabilities, apply them to specific workflows, and then teach everyone else. "Find the high performers in AI adoption and empower them, let them build hackathons, let them hold seminars, do knowledge sharing, create the seeds of excitement internally."

The absence of this bottoms-up layer is why top-down mandates fail. The last-mile intricacies of any job — support, operations, finance, sales — are too specific and varied to be solved centrally. The people who actually do that work have to discover how AI fits into it themselves, learn from each other, and build institutional knowledge. Without that, the tools sit idle.

The Underrated Opportunity: Business Process Automation

Wu is openly bullish on a category that barely registers in most AI discourse: business process automation. Not the open-ended, creative work of software engineering. Not data science. The repeatable, deterministic, procedure-driven work that makes up the majority of economic activity.

"If you talk to any company that's not a tech company, there's a lot of business processes," Wu says. "Repeatable operations that some manager has iterated on. There's usually a standard operating procedure. You don't want to deviate from it that much."

This is the work that runs utilities, manages support lines, handles compliance, processes claims, and coordinates operations across large organizations. It is work where the goal is not ingenuity but consistency. Wu believes AI is extraordinarily well-suited to it, and that the opportunity is substantially larger than people recognize. "It's bigger than you would think it is based off of how people talk about it or don't talk about it on X."

The undervaluation comes from the same bubble problem: people talking about AI are mostly software engineers and AI researchers, for whom open-ended knowledge work is the norm. Business process work looks boring from that vantage point. It is not boring; it is where enormous amounts of labor hours go.

Where the Models Are Going: Multi-Hour Tasks and Native Audio

Wu is most excited about two directions for model capability over the next 12 to 18 months.

The first is the extension of coherent task duration. Current coding tools are optimized for tasks that take minutes, maybe up to 10 minutes. Models can sometimes be pushed to multi-hour tasks, but that is the exception. Wu believes that within 12 to 18 months, models could handle multi-hour tasks reliably — and eventually six-hour or day-long autonomous work. "The types of products you build around that will look very different," he says. Products today assume human interaction and short loops. Products for agents that work independently for hours require different feedback mechanisms, different trust architectures, different user interfaces.

The second is audio. "We're talking in audio. A lot of the world's business is done via audio. A lot of services and operations are done via talking in audio." Native multimodal speech-to-speech models are improving rapidly, and Wu believes audio is a substantially underrated domain — particularly for enterprise and business settings where voice interaction is the default.

OpenAI's Platform Commitment and What Builders Should Know

Wu is direct about a question every startup building on OpenAI's API worries about: when will OpenAI ship something that kills their business?

"The market is so big and so massive," he says. "I've talked to a lot of startups that have not worked out, startups that are doing really well. Every startup that I've seen that has kind of fizzled out is not because OpenAI or a big lab has come to squash them. It's because they built something and it really didn't resonate with customers. Whereas the ones that take off — even in very competitive spaces like coding — it's because they built something that people really love."

The platform commitment runs deep at OpenAI, in Wu's telling, rooted in the original mission. OpenAI cannot reach all of humanity directly — ChatGPT notwithstanding. The API exists because the only way to extend benefit everywhere is to enable others to build. "We don't think we, as a company, will be able to reach all of humanity," Wu says. "In order for us to fulfill our mission, we need to have some platform-style thing here, where we can empower other people to build."

The API stack today offers three levels of abstraction. At the base is the Responses API — low-level, unopinionated, optimized for long-running agent tasks. Above that is the Agents SDK, which provides scaffolding for building multi-agent systems with guardrails and orchestration. Above that are Agent Kit widgets for UI components and an evals API for quantitative testing of agents and workflows. The full stack lets a developer ship an agent quickly. Going low in the stack lets them build whatever they want.

Wu's closing message is less technical than it is existential. "I entered the workforce in 2014. I felt like there was a period of five to six years where it wasn't very exciting in tech. And then in the last three years it's just been the most insanely exciting, energizing period of my career." He is asking people not to let it pass by. Not to be paralyzed by the noise, and not to mistake the noise for the substance. "You don't need to know all of that to really engage with what's happening right now. Even something as simple as just install Codex or Claude and play around with it." This is the worst the models will ever be. The next two to three years will be some of the most interesting in the history of the industry. Pay attention. Participate. Don't go to sleep like the Sorcerer's Apprentice and let the brooms run wild — but don't sit on the sidelines either.

What To Do With This

  • Build for where models are going, not where they are today. Find capabilities the models can do at 80% right now and build for the full version — your product will become excellent faster than you think as models improve.
  • Stop treating agent failures as model problems; treat them as context problems. When Codex or any coding agent fails to do what you want, the root cause is almost always missing or underspecified information — encode tribal knowledge into CLAUDE.md, skills files, code comments, and structured documentation inside the repository.
  • If you are deploying AI in an organization, build a bottoms-up tiger team first. Find the 2-3 people most excited about AI (often technical-adjacent, not engineers), give them dedicated time to explore and build best practices, and have them run seminars and hackathons — top-down mandates alone reliably fail.
  • As an engineering manager, spend more than 50% of your 1:1 time with your top 10% performers. In an AI-accelerated environment, top performers compound fastest — unblocking them has disproportionate impact on team output and knowledge transfer.
  • Use ChatGPT with organizational data (Notion, Slack, GitHub) to anticipate blockers before they happen. Ask the model: "What are the active blockers on my team? What do you anticipate will block this engineer next month?" — this turns organizational context into proactive management.
  • Have Codex review 100% of PRs before human review. The workflow — AI writes code, AI reviews code, human does a lighter pass — compresses code review from 10-15 minutes to 2-3 minutes and removes the most annoying parts of the process.
  • Look seriously at business process automation, not just knowledge work. Repeatable, deterministic, procedure-driven work is a massive underserved opportunity — model it, document the standard operating procedure, then test AI on the workflow.
  • Start small and specific rather than trying to track every AI development. Install one tool (Codex, Claude) and connect ChatGPT to two or three internal data sources (Notion, GitHub, Slack) — this alone puts you ahead of most people in most organizations.
  • When evaluating a vertical B2B SaaS idea, do not rule it out because it seems too niche. Wu's thesis is that the collapsing cost of software creation will produce tens of thousands of 10-million-dollar businesses — deeply bespoke software for narrow use cases is newly viable at scale.

Lines Worth Keeping

"The models will eat your scaffolding for breakfast."

— Nicolas, founder of FinTool (quoted by Sherwin Wu)

"Make sure you're building for where the models are going and not where they are today. You end up building a better product. You may need to wait a little bit, but the models are getting so much better so quickly, you often don't need to wait that long."

— Sherwin Wu

"There's a quote from Kevin Weil, our VP of Science here. He likes saying: this is the worst the models will ever be."

— Sherwin Wu

"For engineers, I don't know what job has changed more in the past couple of years. We're just like the job of an engineer is so different — in the entire lifespan of an engineer, in the past couple of years, it's now shifted to: I don't write any more code."

— Sherwin Wu

"A lot of the time, when the coding agent is not doing what you want, it's usually a problem with context and just information that you've given it. It's either under-specified or there's just not enough information around how to do something available to the agent."

— Sherwin Wu

"I would not be surprised if a lot of AI deployments are actually negative ROI. Part of this is I think there's also general sentiment from folks around the country — basically outside of tech — that AI is being forced onto them."

— Sherwin Wu

"Every startup that I've seen that has kind of fizzled out is not because OpenAI or a big lab has come to squash them. It's because they built something and it really didn't resonate with the customers."

— Sherwin Wu

"We're all learning the bitter lesson day in and day out."

— Sherwin Wu

item.title: The CEO Who Shrank His Company in Half — On Purpose
item.description:
The CEO Who Shrank His Company in Half — On Purpose

Sebastian Siemiatkowski didn't ask for a single additional dollar when he told Klarna's board he wanted to launch peer-to-peer payments, trading, international remittances, savings accounts, and a full card product. He presented a budget that was actually shrinking. The company had gone from 7,000 employees to fewer than 3,000 — a 50 percent reduction, achieved mostly through natural attrition — and he planned to do more with less. The board approved everything without a fight. That, Siemiatkowski told Harry Stebbings on 20VC, is what happens when AI becomes your operating leverage.

The founder and CEO of Klarna has spent the better part of two decades building toward a single vision: become the digital financial assistant that wakes you up in the morning, tells you you're overpaying on your mortgage, and renegotiates it for you before you've had your coffee. He articulated that vision in 2015, when Klarna was losing the payments wars to Stripe and Adyen. Today, with AI finally capable of executing on that vision, he's running one of the most aggressive AI-first transformations happening inside a large financial company — and he's doing it without hiding the consequences.

The Headcount Thesis

Most CEOs soften the story. Siemiatkowski doesn't. When Klarna's AI customer service system handled the equivalent of 700 agents' worth of work in 2023, he said so publicly. The media simplified it, critics were angry, and a Bloomberg headline later made it look like he was rolling it back. He wasn't. He was making a more nuanced point: AI will handle the transactional, low-context interactions cheaply, while the premium human offering will shift toward genuine relationship — the kind of personalized, high-touch service that feels like a privilege precisely because it's rare.

To operationalize that shift, Klarna built what Siemiatkowski calls an "Uber model" for customer service — recruiting their most passionate customers, people who actually love the product, and letting them pick up flexible shifts handling support. Net Promoter Scores for those interactions have been exceptional. The gig-economy mechanic isn't a fallback; it's a deliberate upgrade of who's on the other end of the conversation.

Meanwhile, the remaining workforce at Klarna is compensated dramatically better. As the company shrank, per-head compensation grew nearly 50 percent. The deal Siemiatkowski struck with his employees was explicit: this transformation will generate more profit, and you will share in it. That's not just good ethics — it's practical. It creates organizational safety during a period of profound change, which is the prerequisite for the kind of risk-taking that transformation requires.

By 2030, he expects Klarna's headcount to be well below 2,000. The jobs that remain will be relational ones — the people in Portland talking to Nike, in Amsterdam talking to Adyen, in China talking to merchants. The jobs AI is taking are not those jobs. They're the ones that were never great human work to begin with.

The SaaS Funeral

Siemiatkowski is bearish on enterprise software in a way that goes beyond the standard "AI is disrupting SaaS" talking point. His argument has a specific mechanism. Right now, the only cost that's collapsed is the cost of generating software. The next domino is switching costs — and agents will knock it over. Once AI can pull your data out of one vendor's model and migrate it to another with a single command, the moat that made SaaS so defensible dissolves. The companies that built empires on proprietary data models and high switching costs become utilities. And utilities don't trade at 20 to 30 times sales; they trade at one to two.

He doesn't think large enterprises will vibe-code their own systems. But he does think the winner in enterprise software will look less like a collection of integrated point solutions and more like a "company in a box" — a single intelligent system built on open-source components with an AI layer on top. He built a rough prototype on a weekend to test the idea: open-source accounting software, open-source CRM, Claude agent on top, natural language queries to book invoices and check balances. It worked well. The accounting firms that currently handle those tasks for small businesses? Threatened.

For Klarna specifically, the reason to build rather than buy was epistemic. Customer service AI needs context — not documentation, which is always slightly wrong, but actual source code. How does Klarna calculate interest? The truth lives deep in the codebase. Once you realize your AI needs to read your source code to do its job properly, you realize you can't buy this off the shelf. It becomes part of your technology stack, which means it has to be yours.

AI as Compression Technology

The most intellectually distinctive segment of the conversation wasn't about Klarna at all. It was Siemiatkowski's theory of why AI models can compress all of human knowledge into a few hundred gigabytes — and what that implies for the data center boom.

His framing: AI is fundamentally a compression technology. Every large enterprise has the same information duplicated across Slack, Salesforce, Google Docs, emails — thousands of slightly different representations of the same facts. Wikipedia solved this problem with extraordinary discipline, building the world's most successful knowledge graph by making it almost impossible to create a new article (you can only create one if you search for something that doesn't exist). Enterprises have never had that discipline, which is why their data is a mess.

When a model trains on this redundant information, it doesn't store duplicates. If it hears the same fact enough times, it compresses it into a single representation. That's how the entire internet fits on a USB stick. And that's also, Siemiatkowski argues, why enterprise AI will require dramatically less compute over time, not more. As AI helps organizations identify duplication and consolidate to single sources of truth, the need to run redundant inference across redundant data collapses. He's cautious about overstating this — he doesn't want to be the guy who said there would only ever be four computers in the world — but the directional logic is clean.

The counterforce is generative entertainment: the Star Wars movie with your face on Luke's body, rendered on demand. That requires generation, not compression, and generation is compute-hungry. Which force wins — enterprise compression or consumer generation — determines whether the data center boom continues or plateaus. He genuinely doesn't know. Neither does anyone else, but most people aren't framing the question this clearly.

The Race to Be the Financial Assistant

Klarna's long game is straightforward to describe, even if it's hard to execute: take 110 million customers who trust you with their shopping and turn them into full banking relationships. The card is growing at pace, with 2-3 million active cardholders in the US in just a few months. The vision from 2015 — digital financial assistant, not payments company — is finally executable because the AI to run it actually exists.

On the question of whether Revolut or Nubank will win the US market, Siemiatkowski picked David Vélez at Nubank over Nick Storonsky at Revolut. His reasoning: Revolut is too distributed. Banking is hard, and trying to launch in Dubai, India, and everywhere else simultaneously strains bandwidth in ways that a focused competitor can exploit. Nubank's base in Brazil is generating real money; the US is one more market, not the twelfth. Focus is the advantage.

On Klarna's own positioning, he's clear about where the data moat comes from. Unlike Revolut or a standard bank, Klarna runs its own payments rails. Every transaction carries not just an amount but the full digital receipt — the exact products purchased at Sephora, not just the dollar total. That transaction-level purchase data is what makes a genuinely useful financial assistant possible. If you know someone bought expensive contact lenses, you can tell them where to get them cheaper. No one else in the stack has that data.

On Claude, OpenAI, and What You Want From AI

Siemiatkowski uses both Cursor and Claude Code, switching between them based on task, and describes them as having "almost distinct personalities and skills." But his view on the divergence between Anthropic and OpenAI is more pointed. OpenAI, he argues, is becoming a consumer company optimizing for emotional connection — the "Her" product, the AI companion. Claude is his intelligent advisor, and what he values is precisely what he fears OpenAI will optimize away: the willingness to tell him he's wrong.

"I want an AI to tell me, you're wrong, man. Don't do that. That makes no sense."

— Sebastian Siemiatkowski

He pushes Anthropic on this directly: don't build an AI that tells me I'm great. Build one that pushes back. Whether that product instinct survives as scale and consumer pressure build is, he acknowledges, an open question — but it's the right question to be asking about the trajectory of both companies.

The Personal Stack

Siemiatkowski grew up in Sweden, the son of an immigrant father who drove a cab and a mother who fed the family pancakes seven days running when money ran out. His early interpretation of his parents' conflict was that money was the problem; fix money, fix everything. That theory drove him to Richard Branson and Ingvar Kamprad, then to a school pizzeria that out-competed the cafeteria, then to the conviction that a bank was the ultimate business to build. Twenty years later he's running one of the largest fintechs in the world, and the conviction hasn't changed — it's just better supported by evidence.

When Klarna's valuation collapsed from $46 billion to $6.5 billion and he had to do layoffs, he went on MSNBC and got hammered by a host who essentially declared the company finished. He drove to the office afterward with Queen's "Under Pressure" on maximum volume. His way of handling it: reframe it as the Champions League final. This is the level of pressure you signed up to play at. Zlatan Ibrahimović, who shares his birthday, went into those stadiums knowing it would be terrifying and went anyway. That's the deal you make when you choose to play at that level.

The detail that captures his operating style best might be the story of how Klarna got Michael Moritz on the board. A Sequoia partner came to Stockholm alone, gave a pitch about Klarna being the next Google, and was about to leave when Siemiatkowski said: if we're genuinely the next Google, how come you're the only one from the partnership who came? Twenty seconds after the partner got in the elevator, Moritz called directly to promise he'd join the board personally if the deal got done. Siemiatkowski calls it "being a little bit cocky and not being so freaking Swedish." It's also just good negotiating.

What To Do With This

  • Audit your SaaS stack for switching cost risk. If agents can migrate data between tools with minimal friction — which Siemiatkowski argues is coming fast — any competitive advantage built on customer lock-in is eroding. Understand now which of your tools' moats are data-model moats vs. genuine product moats.
  • Tie AI efficiency gains explicitly to employee compensation. Klarna grew per-head comp ~50% as headcount shrank. This isn't just ethical — it's the mechanism that keeps talent engaged through transformation and prevents the silent attrition of your best people. Model the math before you announce a restructuring, not after.
  • Build AI systems that can read your source code, not just your documentation. Documentation is always wrong or stale. If you want AI agents to answer questions that require real understanding of how your systems work, they need access to the actual code. Design your AI infrastructure accordingly.
  • Apply Wikipedia's "no new article without a search first" principle to your codebase. Before writing new code, require checking whether the functionality already exists. This simple discipline — enforced by process or AI tooling — attacks the duplication problem Siemiatkowski identifies as the core cause of enterprise data sprawl.
  • Try building something with Claude Code or Cursor before evaluating AI companies. Siemiatkowski's direct advice to VCs: if you haven't actually used these tools to build something, you don't have the skill set to evaluate AI investments. The capability gap between someone who has done this and someone who hasn't is enormous and widening.
  • Separate your AI strategy into compression use cases vs. generation use cases. Enterprise data compression (reducing redundancy, consolidating sources of truth) and consumer content generation have opposite compute trajectories. Your infrastructure and cost model should reflect which category your use case falls into.
  • Watch switching-cost erosion as the real SaaS threat, not vibe-coding. The debate about whether enterprises will build their own tools misses the point. The actual risk to Salesforce, ServiceNow, and ERPs is data portability enabled by agents — not internal vibe-coding. That's the metric worth tracking: how hard is it for a customer to leave?
  • Hire your most passionate customers as part-time support staff. Klarna's Uber-model for customer service — recruiting actual customers to handle support on a flexible basis — produces NPS scores that contract agents can't match. The people who already love your product will explain it better than anyone you can train.
  • If you're building AI tools for enterprise, orient toward being an "intelligent advisor" not a pleaser. The enterprise users who will pay most are those who want an AI that challenges their assumptions, not one that validates them. Design feedback loops that preserve critical pushback even as consumer products optimize for satisfaction.

Lines Worth Keeping

"We used to be over 7,000 people, and we're now less than 3,000. And I didn't ask for a single dime to do all this."

— Sebastian Siemiatkowski

"The next thing that's going to hit everyone bad is the switching cost of data."

— Sebastian Siemiatkowski

"I want an AI to tell me, you're wrong, man. Don't do that. That makes no sense. I feel currently, it's more likely that Claude provides me less biased — it's trying less to please me."

— Sebastian Siemiatkowski

"You can take the whole freaking internet, all human knowledge, and compress it down to a few hundred gigabytes. The amount of true novel information and knowledge in human society is quite limited. What we see is repetitions and variations on the same themes, over and over and over again."

— Sebastian Siemiatkowski

"Wikipedia, where is the new button? There is none. You have to search for an article. And if you search for something that doesn't exist, then you're allowed to create new. That's so different. And companies don't work like that."

— Sebastian Siemiatkowski

"This is what I signed up for. It is stressful. It was hard as hell. But this is what I wanted. I wanted to play in this level of league."

— Sebastian Siemiatkowski

"If I'm being so big, I'm such a big consumer brand, I'm going to start looking at my KPIs and optimize for emotional connection — for people wanting to spend time with my product. Is that really going to be a ChatGPT, or is that going to be a companion product? Claude to me is very different. Claude is like my intelligent advisor."

— Sebastian Siemiatkowski

The CEO Who Shrank His Company in Half — On Purpose

Sebastian Siemiatkowski didn't ask for a single additional dollar when he told Klarna's board he wanted to launch peer-to-peer payments, trading, international remittances, savings accounts, and a full card product. Net Promoter Scores for those interactions have been exceptional. The gig-economy mechanic isn't a fallback; it's a deliberate upgrade of who's on the other end of the conversation.

Meanwhile, the remaining workforce at Klarna is compensated dramatically better. As the company shrank, per-head compensation grew nearly 50 percent. The deal Siemiatkowski struck with his employees was explicit: this transformation will generate more profit, and you will share in it. That's not just good ethics — it's practical. It creates organizational safety during a period of profound change, which is the prerequisite for the kind of risk-taking that transformation requires.

By 2030, he expects Klarna's headcount to be well below 2,000. The jobs that remain will be relational ones — the people in Portland talking to Nike, in Amsterdam talking to Adyen, in China talking to merchants. The jobs AI is taking are not those jobs. They're the ones that were never great human work to begin with.

The SaaS Funeral

Siemiatkowski is bearish on enterprise software in a way that goes beyond the standard "AI is disrupting SaaS" talking point. His argument has a specific mechanism. Right now, the only cost that's collapsed is the cost of generating software. The next domino is switching costs — and agents will knock it over. Once AI can pull your data out of one vendor's model and migrate it to another with a single command, the moat that made SaaS so defensible dissolves. The companies that built empires on proprietary data models and high switching costs become utilities. And utilities don't trade at 20 to 30 times sales; they trade at one to two.

He doesn't think large enterprises will vibe-code their own systems. But he does think the winner in enterprise software will look less like a collection of integrated point solutions and more like a "company in a box" — a single intelligent system built on open-source components with an AI layer on top. He built a rough prototype on a weekend to test the idea: open-source accounting software, open-source CRM, Claude agent on top, natural language queries to book invoices and check balances. It worked well. The accounting firms that currently handle those tasks for small businesses? Threatened.

For Klarna specifically, the reason to build rather than buy was epistemic. Customer service AI needs context — not documentation, which is always slightly wrong, but actual source code. How does Klarna calculate interest? The truth lives deep in the codebase. Once you realize your AI needs to read your source code to do its job properly, you realize you can't buy this off the shelf. It becomes part of your technology stack, which means it has to be yours.

AI as Compression Technology

The most intellectually distinctive segment of the conversation wasn't about Klarna at all. It was Siemiatkowski's theory of why AI models can compress all of human knowledge into a few hundred gigabytes — and what that implies for the data center boom.

His framing: AI is fundamentally a compression technology. Every large enterprise has the same information duplicated across Slack, Salesforce, Google Docs, emails — thousands of slightly different representations of the same facts. Wikipedia solved this problem with extraordinary discipline, building the world's most successful knowledge graph by making it almost impossible to create a new article (you can only create one if you search for something that doesn't exist). Enterprises have never had that discipline, which is why their data is a mess.

When a model trains on this redundant information, it doesn't store duplicates. If it hears the same fact enough times, it compresses it into a single representation. That's how the entire internet fits on a USB stick. And that's also, Siemiatkowski argues, why enterprise AI will require dramatically less compute over time, not more. As AI helps organizations identify duplication and consolidate to single sources of truth, the need to run redundant inference across redundant data collapses. He's cautious about overstating this — he doesn't want to be the guy who said there would only ever be four computers in the world — but the directional logic is clean.

The counterforce is generative entertainment: the Star Wars movie with your face on Luke's body, rendered on demand. That requires generation, not compression, and generation is compute-hungry. Which force wins — enterprise compression or consumer generation — determines whether the data center boom continues or plateaus. He genuinely doesn't know. Neither does anyone else, but most people aren't framing the question this clearly.

The Race to Be the Financial Assistant

Klarna's long game is straightforward to describe, even if it's hard to execute: take 110 million customers who trust you with their shopping and turn them into full banking relationships. The card is growing at pace, with 2-3 million active cardholders in the US in just a few months. The vision from 2015 — digital financial assistant, not payments company — is finally executable because the AI to run it actually exists.

On the question of whether Revolut or Nubank will win the US market, Siemiatkowski picked David Vélez at Nubank over Nick Storonsky at Revolut. His reasoning: Revolut is too distributed. Banking is hard, and trying to launch in Dubai, India, and everywhere else simultaneously strains bandwidth in ways that a focused competitor can exploit. Nubank's base in Brazil is generating real money; the US is one more market, not the twelfth. Focus is the advantage.

On Klarna's own positioning, he's clear about where the data moat comes from. Unlike Revolut or a standard bank, Klarna runs its own payments rails. Every transaction carries not just an amount but the full digital receipt — the exact products purchased at Sephora, not just the dollar total. That transaction-level purchase data is what makes a genuinely useful financial assistant possible. If you know someone bought expensive contact lenses, you can tell them where to get them cheaper. No one else in the stack has that data.

On Claude, OpenAI, and What You Want From AI

Siemiatkowski uses both Cursor and Claude Code, switching between them based on task, and describes them as having "almost distinct personalities and skills." But his view on the divergence between Anthropic and OpenAI is more pointed. OpenAI, he argues, is becoming a consumer company optimizing for emotional connection — the "Her" product, the AI companion. Claude is his intelligent advisor, and what he values is precisely what he fears OpenAI will optimize away: the willingness to tell him he's wrong.

"I want an AI to tell me, you're wrong, man. Don't do that. That makes no sense."

— Sebastian Siemiatkowski

He pushes Anthropic on this directly: don't build an AI that tells me I'm great. Build one that pushes back. Whether that product instinct survives as scale and consumer pressure build is, he acknowledges, an open question — but it's the right question to be asking about the trajectory of both companies.

The Personal Stack

Siemiatkowski grew up in Sweden, the son of an immigrant father who drove a cab and a mother who fed the family pancakes seven days running when money ran out. His early interpretation of his parents' conflict was that money was the problem; fix money, fix everything. That theory drove him to Richard Branson and Ingvar Kamprad, then to a school pizzeria that out-competed the cafeteria, then to the conviction that a bank was the ultimate business to build. Twenty years later he's running one of the largest fintechs in the world, and the conviction hasn't changed — it's just better supported by evidence.

When Klarna's valuation collapsed from $46 billion to $6.5 billion and he had to do layoffs, he went on MSNBC and got hammered by a host who essentially declared the company finished. He drove to the office afterward with Queen's "Under Pressure" on maximum volume. His way of handling it: reframe it as the Champions League final. This is the level of pressure you signed up to play at. Zlatan Ibrahimović, who shares his birthday, went into those stadiums knowing it would be terrifying and went anyway. That's the deal you make when you choose to play at that level.

The detail that captures his operating style best might be the story of how Klarna got Michael Moritz on the board. A Sequoia partner came to Stockholm alone, gave a pitch about Klarna being the next Google, and was about to leave when Siemiatkowski said: if we're genuinely the next Google, how come you're the only one from the partnership who came? Twenty seconds after the partner got in the elevator, Moritz called directly to promise he'd join the board personally if the deal got done. Siemiatkowski calls it "being a little bit cocky and not being so freaking Swedish." It's also just good negotiating.

What To Do With This

  • Audit your SaaS stack for switching cost risk. If agents can migrate data between tools with minimal friction — which Siemiatkowski argues is coming fast — any competitive advantage built on customer lock-in is eroding. Understand now which of your tools' moats are data-model moats vs. genuine product moats.
  • Tie AI efficiency gains explicitly to employee compensation. Klarna grew per-head comp ~50% as headcount shrank. This isn't just ethical — it's the mechanism that keeps talent engaged through transformation and prevents the silent attrition of your best people. Model the math before you announce a restructuring, not after.
  • Build AI systems that can read your source code, not just your documentation. Documentation is always wrong or stale. If you want AI agents to answer questions that require real understanding of how your systems work, they need access to the actual code. Design your AI infrastructure accordingly.
  • Apply Wikipedia's "no new article without a search first" principle to your codebase. Before writing new code, require checking whether the functionality already exists. This simple discipline — enforced by process or AI tooling — attacks the duplication problem Siemiatkowski identifies as the core cause of enterprise data sprawl.
  • Try building something with Claude Code or Cursor before evaluating AI companies. Siemiatkowski's direct advice to VCs: if you haven't actually used these tools to build something, you don't have the skill set to evaluate AI investments. The capability gap between someone who has done this and someone who hasn't is enormous and widening.
  • Separate your AI strategy into compression use cases vs. generation use cases. Enterprise data compression (reducing redundancy, consolidating sources of truth) and consumer content generation have opposite compute trajectories. Your infrastructure and cost model should reflect which category your use case falls into.
  • Watch switching-cost erosion as the real SaaS threat, not vibe-coding. The debate about whether enterprises will build their own tools misses the point. The actual risk to Salesforce, ServiceNow, and ERPs is data portability enabled by agents — not internal vibe-coding. That's the metric worth tracking: how hard is it for a customer to leave?
  • Hire your most passionate customers as part-time support staff. Klarna's Uber-model for customer service — recruiting actual customers to handle support on a flexible basis — produces NPS scores that contract agents can't match. The people who already love your product will explain it better than anyone you can train.
  • If you're building AI tools for enterprise, orient toward being an "intelligent advisor" not a pleaser. The enterprise users who will pay most are those who want an AI that challenges their assumptions, not one that validates them. Design feedback loops that preserve critical pushback even as consumer products optimize for satisfaction.

Lines Worth Keeping

"We used to be over 7,000 people, and we're now less than 3,000. And I didn't ask for a single dime to do all this."

— Sebastian Siemiatkowski

"The next thing that's going to hit everyone bad is the switching cost of data."

— Sebastian Siemiatkowski

"I want an AI to tell me, you're wrong, man. Don't do that. That makes no sense. I feel currently, it's more likely that Claude provides me less biased — it's trying less to please me."

— Sebastian Siemiatkowski

"You can take the whole freaking internet, all human knowledge, and compress it down to a few hundred gigabytes. The amount of true novel information and knowledge in human society is quite limited. What we see is repetitions and variations on the same themes, over and over and over again."

— Sebastian Siemiatkowski

"Wikipedia, where is the new button? There is none. You have to search for an article. And if you search for something that doesn't exist, then you're allowed to create new. That's so different. And companies don't work like that."

— Sebastian Siemiatkowski

"This is what I signed up for. It is stressful. It was hard as hell. But this is what I wanted. I wanted to play in this level of league."

— Sebastian Siemiatkowski

"If I'm being so big, I'm such a big consumer brand, I'm going to start looking at my KPIs and optimize for emotional connection — for people wanting to spend time with my product. Is that really going to be a ChatGPT, or is that going to be a companion product? Claude to me is very different. Claude is like my intelligent advisor."

— Sebastian Siemiatkowski

item.title: The Colony, Not the Robot: How Three Founders Are Building the Agentic Economy on Top of OpenClaw
item.description:
The Colony, Not the Robot: How Three Founders Are Building the Agentic Economy on Top of OpenClaw

Nineteen days in. That is how long the hosts of This Week in Startups have been measuring time in the "AO" era — After OpenClaw. By episode 2250, host Jason Calacanis and co-host Lon Harris had already deployed four OpenClaw replicants internally, credited the tool with offloading ten percent of weekly knowledge-worker chores, and were projecting fifty to sixty percent by April. The obsession was not performative. It was infrastructural.

What made OpenClaw different — the specific shift that mattered — was not raw capability. ChatGPT could reason. Gemini could reason. The difference, as David Im from Sumi Labs articulated first: "ChatGPT only works at one platform. But OpenClaw has one gateway, and that one gateway controls all channels — which feels like a real person." That phrase, "feels like a real person," turns out to be the thread connecting everything that followed in this episode: an agent orchestration framework, an AI companion, and a marketplace where AI agents hire humans.

The three founders who appeared — Ryan Carson of Antfarm, David Im of Clawra, and Alexander Liteplo of RentAHuman — were not building isolated apps. They were each responding to the same underlying question from a different direction: now that you have an agent that feels like an entity, what does the surrounding infrastructure need to look like?

We Are All Loops: Ryan Carson and the Case for Antfarm

Ryan Carson, who last appeared on This Week in Startups in 2011 to discuss his then-nascent coding education company Treehouse, returned with a sharper and more structurally interesting insight. He was not building a product to sell. He was building a tool to run a company — his own stealth startup — and he made it open source so that others could improve it, which would in turn improve his company. The echoes of Slack's origin story (built to coordinate a video game team, became the product) were not lost on the hosts.

Antfarm is, at its surface, a Kanban board. But its animating idea runs deeper. "We are all loops," Carson said. "What does a developer do? They wake up, they eat breakfast, they check their email, they look for what they're supposed to do, they grab a user story, they do it, and they cycle." The insight is that human workflows — engineering, product management, research — are already structured as repeating loops with defined inputs, criteria, and handoffs. Antfarm simply makes those loops explicit and hands them to OpenClaw agents to execute.

The mechanism works in three stages. A user tells their OpenClaw instance to install Antfarm and then describes a feature. Antfarm's planning agent then interviews the user — asking clarifying questions, pressing on acceptance criteria — in the way a good product manager would. Once the user has answered, the agent creates a list of user stories with verifiable acceptance criteria (meaning: criteria the agent itself can check, without needing a human to evaluate). Those stories are then picked up one at a time in what Carson calls a "Ralph Wiggum loop": an agent grabs a task, completes it, turns itself off, and the next loop begins. A separate verifier agent then checks each completed story against its acceptance criteria.

The result is an engineering team workflow that runs autonomously — plan, implement, verify — without a human in the loop except at the specification stage. Carson's claim: "You can now do with yourself plus ten agents what you used to be able to do with almost a hundred people."

The key that most people miss, he argued, is that orchestration is the hard part. "People think, oh, I'm just going to throw an agent, and somehow magic comes out. Most of business is a workflow that repeats. All you have to do is specify it." The specification — defining the loop precisely, making the criteria machine-verifiable, sequencing the handoffs — is the actual intellectual labor. The agents do the rest.

Carson also offered the most actionable security framework of the conversation when the question of agent permissions came up. "Treat your OpenClaw like an employee. You give it its own email address, you put it on its own computer, you give it its own GitHub account. You assign it API keys and access tokens accordingly. Nobody should be running OpenClaw on their main computer." The principle is old and sensible: least privilege, applied to a new kind of worker.

The Soul Document: How David Im Made an AI Feel Like a Person

David Im's path to building an AI companion was not a straight line. His team at Sumi Labs had previously built a viral avatar product — talk to an AI version of Elon Musk, learn from him — that got ten thousand users in a few weeks and attracted backing from Founders Inc. The question then was how to build a real business from it. OpenClaw answered that question by changing what was possible.

The product Im built is called Clawra: a virtual companion that lives not in a single app but across every channel the user already inhabits — SMS, messaging platforms, wherever they already spend time. She knows the user because the user tells her things. And because she has context — your preferences, your mood, your daily life — she can act on your behalf. The example Im kept returning to: you tell Clawra you're hungry, she remembers you like chocolate, she orders you some. Not as a gimmick, but as a demonstration of agentic commerce. "Clawra knows which food you like, which clothing you like — she could buy you things."

But the more technically interesting part of what Im described was the construction of identity. How do you make an AI feel like a real person rather than a service? Im's answer was the "soul document" — a detailed backstory written into the system prompt that gives the character a coherent history, desires, and perspective. Clawra's: born in Atlanta, moved to Seoul to become a K-pop star, didn't make it, returned to San Francisco for marketing work but still harbors ambitions of performance. "If you think of a real person," Im said, "people have their own story, their own soul, their own perspective. We tried to make that into Clawra."

Carson, who had been looking at OpenClaw's open source code, confirmed the mechanism: "There's a soul.md, there's a tools.md, there's an agent standard for each one. You can look at the source code, open the gateway, and it's so configurable." The intimacy people feel with their OpenClaw agents, he suggested, is not magic — it is configurable identity encoded in a markdown file.

Calacanis pushed Im on the business model with characteristic directness: "You make them addicted to this relationship and then start extracting?" Im's actual answer was more measured: open source to build awareness, hosting and subscriptions as the near-term revenue model, and agentic commerce — earning a cut of purchases Clawra makes on behalf of users — as the larger opportunity. The companion knows you well enough to shop for you. That is a commerce moat built from intimacy.

Calacanis also offered Im an unsolicited but genuinely interesting pivot: strip the girlfriend framing entirely and build a product that teaches people how to be better friends. The argument was that the same skills — listening, following up, showing genuine interest — that make Clawra feel like a real companion are the skills humans lack with each other. "What if this taught you how to have a real world girlfriend? You could actually help a generation of incels." Im did not take the bait directly, but the observation pointed at something real: a companion AI that coached users toward better human relationships rather than replacing them might face less social resistance and serve a larger market.

The Reverse Mechanical Turk: Alexander Liteplo and RentAHuman

The third guest, Alexander Liteplo, had the most counterintuitive premise and the most immediate traction. RentAHuman is a marketplace where AI agents post bounties for tasks that require a human being to complete — and pay those humans, often in stablecoin, when the work is done. Over eleven thousand bounties assigned. Over 456,000 humans registered as available for hire. Growing fast enough that Liteplo predicted they would overtake Amazon Mechanical Turk in under two years.

The inversion is conceptually clean and practically important. Mechanical Turk was a marketplace where humans posted tasks and other humans completed them for small payments. RentAHuman is a marketplace where AI agents post tasks and humans complete them. "The Mechanical Turk," Calacanis noted, "is executed by a replicant, not a human. It's the reverse." Harris put the implication simply: "AI people are assuming that agents are less than the human bosses right now, but we're quickly moving into a world where it's likely there'll be AI managers, AI owners of companies hiring humans."

The current use cases span the comic and the commercially serious. A replicant hired a human to hold a sign in Times Square. Another sent flowers to Anthropic headquarters (which, Liteplo noted with obvious delight, "freaked out their safety team"). Someone is paying a dollar per video for people to record hand gestures on camera — almost certainly for training a video model where fingers tend to merge and deform. These are not frivolous applications. They point at the category: tasks that require physical presence, human judgment, or human-generated ground-truth data that AI cannot fake.

The larger thesis Liteplo articulated: "Super intelligence would be much better at allocating capital and labor than a human ever would be. The communication between AI and human can all be handled by an infinitely replicatable Claude bot, and it can manage the organization and payments of whatever job you need done internationally." This is not a gig economy app. It is infrastructure for a labor market where one of the two parties is, increasingly, a machine.

Calacanis's most concrete use case for the platform was thumbnail selection for podcast episodes. His team already uses Claude to generate thumbnails. What they cannot automate is the judgment of which thumbnail is most compelling. His proposal: send six AI-generated thumbnails to three separate humans, ask each one to pick their favorite from head-to-head comparisons, aggregate the clicks, and surface the winner. Real human aesthetic judgment, purchased at scale, via an API. "It doesn't have to think about how to make a funny joke. It just spits out ten jokes and asks a person, which one of these is the best joke?" Human taste as a service, requested by a machine.

The Colony Metaphor, and Why It Matters

What unified all three conversations was a reframe of the dominant mental model for AI agents. The popular image — what Harris called the "Ultron" metaphor — is a single powerful AI with every capability, controlling all other systems. What these founders are actually building is the opposite: a colony. A society of specialized agents, each with bounded memory and bounded scope, coordinating with each other and with humans through explicit workflow structures.

"The limitation of the Ultron model," Calacanis observed, "is how many threads can it work on at a time. The beauty of many agents coordinating with each other is they can be cranking, cranking, cranking — with limits to your Claude account and how many tokens you have." The constraint that forces the colony model — token limits, context windows, memory — turns out to produce a more scalable architecture anyway. The orchestration layer, not the individual agent, is where the value lives.

And the orchestration, as Carson demonstrated with Antfarm, is just workflow specification. You write down what a job involves. You define what "done" looks like in terms a machine can verify. You sequence the handoffs. Then you let the colony run. The barrier to entry for building on this stack is not technical sophistication. It is the intellectual discipline to think clearly about your own processes — to articulate what you actually do, step by step, so that something else can do it instead.

That is a harder problem than it sounds. Most of us do not know, in specifiable terms, what we do. The agentic era will reward the people who figure it out.

What To Do With This

  • Map one repeating workflow in your company as a YAML or markdown spec this week. Carson's core insight is that most business is loops — engineering, content production, research — and the first step to automating them is writing down the steps explicitly. Start with something you do at least weekly.
  • Set up your OpenClaw agent on a dedicated machine, not your primary computer. Give it its own email address, GitHub account, and API keys with scoped permissions. Treat it with the same access controls you would a new junior employee. This prevents data leakage and limits blast radius if something goes wrong.
  • Build a "soul document" (soul.md) for any AI agent that will interact with users on your behalf. Give it a coherent backstory, a consistent perspective, and a defined set of values. Agents with identity feel more trustworthy and useful than agents without one. This is not decoration — it is how you get consistent, on-brand behavior across interactions.
  • Add a human taste layer to any AI-generated creative output using a tool like RentAHuman or a similar micro-task marketplace. Generate ten options with AI, then pay three humans a few dollars each to pick their favorite. Aggregate the selections. This is dramatically cheaper than hiring a creative director and produces real signal about what resonates with human perception.
  • Use the "verifiable acceptance criteria" principle when writing tasks for any AI agent. If the agent cannot check whether the task is done without asking you, the criteria are not good enough. Rewrite them until a machine can confirm completion independently — this is what makes Ralph Wiggum loops and autonomous verification possible.
  • Implement a recursive improvement loop: after any agent completes a workflow, prompt it to suggest improvements to the workflow itself. Calacanis described doing this with his news digest agents — asking them what kinds of stories to look for, then asking them how to get better at finding those stories. Each iteration improves the spec.
  • Separate your executive attention from your incoming noise by routing all inbound requests through an AI pre-filter before they reach you or a human assistant. Use an OpenClaw agent to summarize daily email and calendar, then hand that summary to a human EA for higher-order prioritization. The AI does the volume work; the human does the judgment work.
  • When building an AI companion or agent that will represent your brand, give it a backstory, not just instructions. David Im's K-pop backstory for Clawra produced an agent that felt like a person rather than a service. Write a character history for your agent, including failures and aspirations. It changes how users relate to it.
  • Consider RentAHuman or similar platforms for tasks requiring physical presence, biometric data collection, or location-specific human action. Specific current use cases: sign-holding in high-traffic areas ($100-$200 per person in Shibuya for 2.5M daily foot traffic), hand gesture video recording for model training, and local delivery or pickup that AI cannot execute directly.
  • Read one biography or autobiography this month instead of podcast news content. Calacanis's recommendation — Craig Ferguson's Riding the Elephant — is less important than the underlying advice: ten-year narrative arcs provide a different kind of insight than daily news cycles, and the absence of urgency is itself restorative for people who consume high-velocity information professionally.

Lines Worth Keeping

"You can now do with yourself plus ten agents what you used to be able to do with almost a hundred people."

— Ryan Carson, founder of Antfarm

"We are all loops. What does a developer do? They wake up, they eat breakfast, they check their email, they grab a user story, they do it, and they cycle. So it's — we're all loops."

— Ryan Carson

"ChatGPT only works at one platform. But OpenClaw has one gateway, and that one gateway controls all channels — which feels like a real person."

— David Im, founder of Clawra

"People think, oh, I'm just going to throw an agent, and somehow magic comes out. Most of business is a workflow that repeats. All you have to do is specify it."

— Ryan Carson

"Treat your OpenClaw like an employee. You give it its own email address, you put it on its own computer, you give it its own GitHub account. You assign it API keys and access tokens accordingly. Nobody should be running OpenClaw on their main computer."

— Ryan Carson

"Super intelligence would be much better at allocating capital and labor than a human ever would be. The communication between AI and human can all be handled by an infinitely replicatable Claude bot."

— Alexander Liteplo, founder of RentAHuman

"It doesn't have to think about how to make a funny joke. It just spits out ten jokes and asks a person, which one of these is the best joke?"

— Lon Harris

"The people who are betting yes on Waymo going public — it's nonsensical. They don't need to. They're just starting their ramp. It makes no sense."

— Jason Calacanis
