His team at Sumi Labs had previously built a viral avatar product — talk to an AI version of Elon Musk, learn from him — that got ten thousand users in a few weeks and attracted backing from Founders Inc. The question then was how to build a real business from it. OpenClaw answered that question by changing what was possible.

The product Im built is called Clawra: a virtual companion that lives not in a single app but across every channel the user already inhabits — SMS, messaging platforms, wherever they already spend time. She knows the user because the user tells her things. And because she has context — your preferences, your mood, your daily life — she can act on your behalf. The example Im kept returning to: you tell Clawra you're hungry, she remembers you like chocolate, she orders you some. Not as a gimmick, but as a demonstration of agentic commerce. "Clawra knows which food you like, which clothing you like — she could buy you things."

But the more technically interesting part of what Im described was the construction of identity. How do you make an AI feel like a real person rather than a service? Im's answer was the "soul document" — a detailed backstory written into the system prompt that gives the character a coherent history, desires, and perspective. Clawra's: born in Atlanta, moved to Seoul to become a K-pop star, didn't make it, returned to San Francisco for marketing work but still harbors ambitions of performance. "If you think of a real person," Im said, "people have their own story, their own soul, their own perspective. We tried to make that into Clawra."

Carson, who had been looking at OpenClaw's open source code, confirmed the mechanism: "There's a soul.md, there's a tools.md, there's an agent standard for each one. You can look at the source code, open the gateway, and it's so configurable." The intimacy people feel with their OpenClaw agents, he suggested, is not magic — it is configurable identity encoded in a markdown file.

Calacanis pushed Im on the business model with characteristic directness: "You make them addicted to this relationship and then start extracting?" Im's actual answer was more measured: open source to build awareness, hosting and subscriptions as the near-term revenue model, and agentic commerce — earning a cut of purchases Clawra makes on behalf of users — as the larger opportunity. The companion knows you well enough to shop for you. That is a commerce moat built from intimacy.

Calacanis also offered Im an unsolicited but genuinely interesting pivot: strip the girlfriend framing entirely and build a product that teaches people how to be better friends. The argument was that the same skills — listening, following up, showing genuine interest — that make Clawra feel like a real companion are the skills humans lack with each other. "What if this taught you how to have a real world girlfriend? You could actually help a generation of incels." Im did not take the bait directly, but the observation pointed at something real: a companion AI that coached users toward better human relationships rather than replacing them might face less social resistance and serve a larger market.

The Reverse Mechanical Turk: Alexander Liteplo and RentAHuman

The third guest, Alexander Liteplo, had the most counterintuitive premise and the most immediate traction. RentAHuman is a marketplace where AI agents post bounties for tasks that require a human being to complete — and pay those humans, often in stablecoin, when the work is done. Over eleven thousand bounties assigned. Over 456,000 humans registered as available for hire. Growing fast enough that Liteplo predicted they would overtake Amazon Mechanical Turk in under two years.

The inversion is conceptually clean and practically important. Mechanical Turk was a marketplace where humans posted tasks and other humans completed them for small payments. RentAHuman is a marketplace where AI agents post tasks and humans complete them. "The Mechanical Turk," Calacanis noted, "is executed by a replicant, not a human. It's the reverse." Harris put the implication simply: "AI people are assuming that agents are less than the human bosses right now, but we're quickly moving into a world where it's likely there'll be AI managers, AI owners of companies hiring humans."

The current use cases span the comic and the commercially serious. A replicant hired a human to hold a sign in Times Square. Another sent flowers to Anthropic headquarters (which, Liteplo noted with obvious delight, "freaked out their safety team"). Someone is paying a dollar per video for people to record hand gestures on camera — almost certainly for training a video model where fingers tend to merge and deform. These are not frivolous applications. They point at the category: tasks that require physical presence, human judgment, or human-generated ground-truth data that AI cannot fake.

The larger thesis Liteplo articulated: "Super intelligence would be much better at allocating capital and labor than a human ever would be. The communication between AI and human can all be handled by an infinitely replicatable Claude bot, and it can manage the organization and payments of whatever job you need done internationally." This is not a gig economy app. It is infrastructure for a labor market where one of the two parties is, increasingly, a machine.

Calacanis's most concrete use case for the platform was thumbnail selection for podcast episodes. His team already uses Claude to generate thumbnails. What they cannot automate is the judgment of which thumbnail is most compelling. His proposal: send six AI-generated thumbnails to three separate humans, ask each one to pick their favorite from head-to-head comparisons, aggregate the clicks, and surface the winner. Real human aesthetic judgment, purchased at scale, via an API. "It doesn't have to think about how to make a funny joke. It just spits out ten jokes and asks a person, which one of these is the best joke?" Human taste as a service, requested by a machine.

The Colony Metaphor, and Why It Matters

What unified all three conversations was a reframe of the dominant mental model for AI agents. The popular image — what Harris called the "Ultron" metaphor — is a single powerful AI with every capability, controlling all other systems. What these founders are actually building is the opposite: a colony. A society of specialized agents, each with bounded memory and bounded scope, coordinating with each other and with humans through explicit workflow structures.

"The limitation of the Ultron model," Calacanis observed, "is how many threads can it work on at a time. The beauty of many agents coordinating with each other is they can be cranking, cranking, cranking — with limits to your Claude account and how many tokens you have." The constraint that forces the colony model — token limits, context windows, memory — turns out to produce a more scalable architecture anyway. The orchestration layer, not the individual agent, is where the value lives.

And the orchestration, as Carson demonstrated with Antfarm, is just workflow specification. You write down what a job involves. You define what "done" looks like in terms a machine can verify. You sequence the handoffs. Then you let the colony run. The barrier to entry for building on this stack is not technical sophistication. It is the intellectual discipline to think clearly about your own processes — to articulate what you actually do, step by step, so that something else can do it instead.

That is a harder problem than it sounds. Most of us do not know, in specifiable terms, what we do. The agentic era will reward the people who figure it out.

What To Do With This

  • Map one repeating workflow in your company as a YAML or markdown spec this week. Carson's core insight is that most business is loops — engineering, content production, research — and the first step to automating them is writing down the steps explicitly. Start with something you do at least weekly.
  • Set up your OpenClaw agent on a dedicated machine, not your primary computer. Give it its own email address, GitHub account, and API keys with scoped permissions. Treat it with the same access controls you would a new junior employee. This prevents data leakage and limits blast radius if something goes wrong.
  • Build a "soul document" (soul.md) for any AI agent that will interact with users on your behalf. Give it a coherent backstory, a consistent perspective, and a defined set of values. Agents with identity feel more trustworthy and useful than agents without one. This is not decoration — it is how you get consistent, on-brand behavior across interactions.
  • Add a human taste layer to any AI-generated creative output using a tool like RentAHuman or a similar micro-task marketplace. Generate ten options with AI, then pay three humans a few dollars each to pick their favorite. Aggregate the selections. This is dramatically cheaper than hiring a creative director and produces real signal about what resonates with human perception.
  • Use the "verifiable acceptance criteria" principle when writing tasks for any AI agent. If the agent cannot check whether the task is done without asking you, the criteria are not good enough. Rewrite them until a machine can confirm completion independently — this is what makes Ralph Wiggum loops and autonomous verification possible.
  • Implement a recursive improvement loop: after any agent completes a workflow, prompt it to suggest improvements to the workflow itself. Calacanis described doing this with his news digest agents — asking them what kinds of stories to look for, then asking them how to get better at finding those stories. Each iteration improves the spec.
  • Separate your executive attention from your incoming noise by routing all inbound requests through an AI pre-filter before they reach you or a human assistant. Use an OpenClaw agent to summarize daily email and calendar, then hand that summary to a human EA for higher-order prioritization. The AI does the volume work; the human does the judgment work.
  • When building an AI companion or agent that will represent your brand, give it a backstory, not just instructions. David Im's K-pop backstory for Clawra produced an agent that felt like a person rather than a service. Write a character history for your agent, including failures and aspirations. It changes how users relate to it.
  • Consider RentAHuman or similar platforms for tasks requiring physical presence, biometric data collection, or location-specific human action. Specific current use cases: sign-holding in high-traffic areas ($100-$200 per person in Shibuya for 2.5M daily foot traffic), hand gesture video recording for model training, and local delivery or pickup that AI cannot execute directly.
  • Read one biography or autobiography this month instead of podcast news content. Calacanis's recommendation — Craig Ferguson's Riding the Elephant — is less important than the underlying advice: ten-year narrative arcs provide a different kind of insight than daily news cycles, and the absence of urgency is itself restorative for people who consume high-velocity information professionally.

Lines Worth Keeping

"You can now do with yourself plus ten agents what you used to be able to do with almost a hundred people."

— Ryan Carson, founder of Antfarm

"We are all loops. What does a developer do? They wake up, they eat breakfast, they check their email, they grab a user story, they do it, and they cycle. So it's — we're all loops."

— Ryan Carson

"ChatGPT only works at one platform. But OpenClaw has one gateway, and that one gateway controls all channels — which feels like a real person."

— David Im, founder of Clawra

"People think, oh, I'm just going to throw an agent, and somehow magic comes out. Most of business is a workflow that repeats. All you have to do is specify it."

— Ryan Carson

"Treat your OpenClaw like an employee. You give it its own email address, you put it on its own computer, you give it its own GitHub account. You assign it API keys and access tokens accordingly. Nobody should be running OpenClaw on their main computer."

— Ryan Carson

"Super intelligence would be much better at allocating capital and labor than a human ever would be. The communication between AI and human can all be handled by an infinitely replicatable Claude bot."

— Alexander Liteplo, founder of RentAHuman

"It doesn't have to think about how to make a funny joke. It just spits out ten jokes and asks a person, which one of these is the best joke?"

— Lon Harris

"The people who are betting yes on Waymo going public — it's nonsensical. They don't need to. They're just starting their ramp. It makes no sense."

— Jason Calacanis

item.title: The Most Surprising Thing Is That Nobody Seems to Notice
item.description:
The Most Surprising Thing Is That Nobody Seems to Notice

Dario Amodei has been watching the same exponential curve since 2017. The models have marched roughly where he expected — from smart high school student to smart college student to the early edges of PhD-level work. What he did not expect was the world's collective failure to register what that means. "The most surprising thing has been the lack of public recognition of how close we are to the end of the exponential," he told Dwarkesh Patel. "To me, it is absolutely wild that you have people within the bubble and outside the bubble talking about these tired old hot-button political issues — and around us, we're near the end of the exponential."

That dissonance — between what Amodei sees from inside Anthropic and what the broader world appears to believe — is the animating tension of this conversation. He is not making a modest claim. He thinks we are one to three years from AI systems that can match or exceed a Nobel Prize winner's intellectual output across essentially every domain of digital work. He is 90 percent confident it happens within a decade. And he is spending an extraordinary amount of time trying to get policymakers, economists, and national security professionals to take that seriously before the window to shape the outcome closes.

The Same Hypothesis, Eight Years Running

In 2017, Amodei wrote an internal document he called "The Big Blob of Compute Hypothesis." GPT-1 had just come out. Robotics, language modeling, and game-playing RL were all separate research tracks. His claim, before any of them had clearly converged: only a handful of things actually matter for intelligence at scale. Raw compute. Quantity of data. The breadth of data distribution. Training duration. An objective function that can scale without bound. And numerical stability — the engineering plumbing that lets the blob of compute flow smoothly rather than becoming turbulent.

Every major development since then, he argues, is a footnote to that document. The pre-training scaling laws were the first instantiation. Reinforcement learning turns out to be the second. "With RL, it's actually just the same," he says. "We train the model on math contests, how well the model does is log-linear in how long we've trained it. We see that as well. It's not just math contests. It's a wide variety of RL tasks."

The conceptual parallel runs deeper. Early pre-training on narrow corpora — fan fiction, literary text — produced models that didn't generalize. The shift came when training moved to the full breadth of the internet. Amodei expects the same transition to repeat in RL: early narrow training on math and coding contests will give way to training across thousands of realistic tasks, and generalization will emerge in the same way. The goal was never to cover every specific task. It was to reach the point where the model could cover any task.

Where We Are on the Spectrum — and Why "Diffusion" Is Not a Cop-Out

One of the sharper exchanges in this conversation concerns whether economic diffusion is a meaningful concept or a convenient excuse. Patel pushes hard on this: AIs have structural advantages over human hires — they can read a company's entire Slack history in minutes, can be copied without adverse selection risk, never require onboarding paperwork. So why is diffusion slow?

Amodei's answer is precise. Diffusion is real and measurable, not a rhetorical escape hatch. Anthropic's own revenue trajectory makes the case: roughly $100 million in 2023, $1 billion in 2024, $9–10 billion in 2025, with "another few billion" added in January 2026 alone. That is a 10x-per-year compounding curve, which he acknowledges cannot go on forever — GDP is finite — but which represents adoption velocity faster than any previous technology. The friction is not model capability. It is legal review cycles, security and compliance gates, change management within enterprises, and the latency between a decision-maker understanding the value proposition and actually deploying the tool at scale.

Claude Code, Anthropic's coding agent, is his most concrete example. "There is no reason why a developer at a large enterprise should not be adopting Claude Code as quickly as an individual developer," he says. "We do everything we can to promote it. And we sell Claude Code to enterprises — big financial companies, big pharmaceutical companies — and all of them are adopting it much faster than enterprises typically adopt new technology. But it takes time." Legal must approve. Security and compliance must sign off. Leaders three levels from the technology must understand why the spend is justified.

The productivity gains from coding agents, he estimates, are currently around 15–20 percent total factor speedup — up from roughly 5 percent six months prior. That acceleration is starting to matter competitively. He expects the number to keep climbing.

The Spectrum Nobody Quotes Correctly

Amodei is careful to distinguish between a sequence of milestones that the public frequently conflates. When he said, eight or nine months ago, that AI would be writing 90 percent of lines of code within three to six months, people heard "90 percent fewer software engineers needed." These are not the same claim and are, he says, worlds apart.

The actual spectrum runs from: 90 percent of code lines written by AI → 100 percent of code lines written by AI → 90 percent of end-to-end SWE tasks completed autonomously (including environment setup, compilation, testing, documentation) → 100 percent of today's SWE tasks → net 90 percent reduction in demand for software engineers. Each step is meaningfully different. Each requires a different threshold of model capability. And we are, he argues, traversing the spectrum very quickly — not arriving at the final step tomorrow, but not standing still either.

The question of whether models can learn on the job — building the accumulated context that makes a six-month employee more valuable than a day-one hire — gets a more nuanced treatment. Amodei thinks this may simply not be a real barrier. Pre-training generalization already gives models broader knowledge than any individual human possesses. In-context learning at a million tokens represents weeks of human reading. And continual learning, as an engineering problem rather than a research problem, is likely solvable within one to two years through longer context windows and better inference serving. More importantly, he argues that history in ML is filled with apparent barriers that simply dissolved into the big blob of compute — syntactic versus semantic understanding, statistical correlation versus reasoning, and so on.

The Country of Geniuses and the Spreadsheet Problem

Amodei's working target for "country of geniuses in a data center" — a phrase from his essay "Machines of Loving Grace" — is 2026 or 2027, with one to two years of uncertainty on either side. At that point, AI systems would have the ability to navigate any digital interface a human can use, with intellectual capabilities matching or exceeding Nobel laureates, and the ability to interface with the physical world.

Patel presses the obvious tension: if you genuinely believe that, why isn't Anthropic buying trillions of dollars of compute right now? The answer is a lesson in operating under extreme uncertainty. Even if the technology arrives exactly when Amodei expects, the revenue curve is less certain. If a pharmaceutical company uses AI to discover a new drug, the regulatory process, manufacturing scale-up, and distribution still take years. If you buy the compute for a world where the revenue arrives simultaneously with the capability, and revenue is off by a year, "there's no force on earth that could stop you from going bankrupt." The industry as a whole is building toward multiple trillions of dollars of annual compute by 2028–2029 — roughly 10–15 gigawatts of capacity this year, growing 3x annually. Anthropic is inside that figure, buying what allows them to capture strong upside scenarios without assuming the maximum possible demand materializes on schedule.

On profitability, the model is more elegant than it appears. Each individual deployed model is already profitable at the gross margin level — inference revenue minus inference cost exceeds 50 percent. The company loses money because each training run for the next-generation model costs multiples of what the previous generation cost. As the training scale-up rate stabilizes, the underlying profitability of the business will emerge. Projected 2028 profitability is not evidence of timidity; it is a consequence of prediction error in a hypergrowth environment, where the company will swing between under- and over-provisioned states depending on whether they guessed demand correctly twelve months earlier.

Governance at the Speed of Capability

The safety and governance questions get the same intellectual honesty. Amodei is not reassured by current regulatory activity. The Tennessee bill prohibiting AI from providing emotional support struck him as legislators reacting to surface-level fears without understanding the technology. More troubling was the proposed federal moratorium on state AI regulation — a ten-year pause on state action with no credible plan for federal regulation to fill the gap. "Given the serious dangers I lay out around biological weapons and bioterrorism, autonomy risk, and the timelines we've been talking about — ten years is an eternity. That's a crazy thing to do."

His preferred approach: start with transparency standards now. As risks become more concrete and better-evidenced — which he thinks could happen as early as later this year — move quickly to targeted regulation in the specific domains where risk has materialized. The process must be nimble in a way that legislatures normally are not, which is part of why he is writing public essays, speaking to policymakers directly, and flagging urgency as loudly as he can.

On geopolitics, his concern is about initial conditions. He does not believe one country should dictate the rules of the post-AGI world. But he does believe there will be a window — possibly narrow — where some countries or coalitions have reached critical thresholds of AI capability before others, and that the rules negotiated during that window will shape what comes after. His preference is that democratic nations hold stronger hands at that table, which is why he advocates for export controls on chips to China despite the revenue pressure against it. Growth and economic value, he argues, will come easily in an AI-accelerated world. Distribution of benefits, political freedom, and rights are the things that will actually be hard.

The Asymmetry of Retrospect

The conversation closes with an unusual question: when someone eventually writes the history of this era, what will they most likely miss? Amodei's answer cuts to the heart of his concern. They will miss the genuine contingency of it all — the degree to which, at every point along the exponential, the people inside did not know it was inevitable. "When people look back, it will be hard for them to put themselves in the place of people who were actually making a bet on this thing to happen that wasn't inevitable." The world outside those bets was not acting on them at all. If history renders this period as obvious, it will have gotten the phenomenology completely wrong.

The second thing they will miss is the speed. The decisions that turn out to be most consequential may not have been recognized as consequential when they were made. "Some very critical decision will be some decision that someone just comes into my office and says, Dario, you have two minutes — should we do A or B? And I'm like, I don't know, I have to eat lunch, let's do B. And that ends up being the most consequential thing ever."

That admission — from the CEO of one of the most important companies in the world at what may be one of the most important moments in history — is not false modesty. It is the most honest thing he says.

What To Do With This

  • Treat the 10x-per-year revenue curve as a planning constraint, not a headline. Amodei's framework for data center purchasing — buy enough to capture strong upside without betting on the maximum possible scenario — applies to any organization making multi-year AI infrastructure commitments. Model the demand cone, not just the capability curve.
  • Track AI's productivity contribution as a percentage of total factor output, not lines of code. Amodei estimates coding agents are currently delivering a 15–20% total factor speedup — up from ~5% six months ago. This is the metric that actually predicts competitive advantage, not adoption rates or lines-of-code percentages.
  • Reframe "on-the-job learning" as a context-window engineering problem, not a fundamental barrier. For most knowledge-work tasks, the accumulated context a six-month employee has built can be approximated through a combination of broad pre-training and large context windows. Before assuming a task requires human continuity, test whether stuffing the model's context window with all relevant history achieves comparable results.
  • Build RL training environments across the broadest realistic distribution of task variations, not just the most common scenarios. Amodei's analogy to pre-training generalization is actionable: narrow training (like GPT-1 on fan fiction) produces narrow capability. If you are building agents for a domain, the goal is breadth of scenario coverage, not depth on the median case. Labelbox's approach — building thousands of distinct interaction scenarios across multiple persona types — is the right pattern.
  • Accelerate your own AI adoption now specifically because enterprise procurement cycles are long. Amodei's observation that large enterprises are adopting Claude Code significantly faster than previous technologies while still taking months to complete is a competitive signal. If you are at an enterprise that has not started the legal and security review process for a serious AI deployment, the time cost of waiting is compounding against you.
  • Watch the "verifiable task" frontier as the leading indicator of general capability. Amodei is highly confident about AI performance on tasks with objective metrics (coding, math) and views the gap to non-verifiable tasks (strategic planning, scientific discovery) as closing through generalization. Monitor progress on verifiable benchmarks — particularly end-to-end software engineering tasks — as the clearest signal of when broader capability thresholds are being crossed.
  • Read "Machines of Loving Grace" and "The Adolescence of Technology" as operating frameworks, not thought experiments. These essays contain Amodei's actual probability-weighted views on timelines, governance, geopolitics, and economic impact. They are being shared with policymakers and should inform any organization thinking about AI strategy over a two-to-five year horizon.
  • Build internal communication infrastructure for the pace of change, not for quarterly update cycles. Amodei speaks to Anthropic's 2,500 employees every two weeks with a three-to-four page document covering model launches, product developments, competitive landscape, and geopolitics — then takes live questions. The alternative (filtering everything through six levels of management) destroys organizational coherence under conditions of rapid change. Adopt a similar cadence if you are leading a team through significant AI-driven transformation.
  • Engage now on transparency-first AI regulation before the policy window closes. Amodei's sequencing — transparency standards now, targeted safety regulation as specific risks materialize — is the most actionable near-term policy position. Organizations that want to shape rather than react to AI regulation should be engaged at the transparency standard stage, not waiting for the more restrictive legislation that follows.

Lines Worth Keeping

"The most surprising thing has been the lack of public recognition of how close we are to the end of the exponential. To me, it is absolutely wild that you have people talking about these same tired old hot-button political issues — and around us, we're near the end of the exponential."

— Dario Amodei
"I'm at like 90% on that. It's hard to go much higher than 90% because the world is so unpredictable. Maybe the irreducible uncertainty is things like multiple companies have internal turmoil, Taiwan gets invaded, and all the fabs get blown up by missiles."

— Dario Amodei, on his confidence that AGI arrives within 10 years
"There is zero time for bullshit. There is zero time for feeling like we're productive when we're not. Like, we see the end productivity every few months in the form of model launches. There's no kidding yourself about this."

— Dario Amodei, on productivity measurement inside Anthropic
"I think everything we've seen so far is compatible with the idea that there's one fast exponential that's the capability of the model, and then there's another fast exponential that's downstream of that, which is the diffusion of the model into the economy. Not instant, not slow, much faster than any previous technology, but it has its limits."

— Dario Amodei
"What will not come easily is distribution of benefits, distribution of wealth, political freedom. These are the things that are going to be hard to achieve. When I think about policy, I think the technology and the market will deliver all the fundamental benefits almost faster than we can take them."

— Dario Amodei
"When people look back, it will be hard for them to put themselves in the place of people who were actually making a bet on this thing to happen that wasn't inevitable — that there's a world outside us that's not acting on that at all. And that's one of the things I'm trying to change."

— Dario Amodei
"Some very critical decision will be some decision that someone just comes into my office and says, Dario, you have two minutes — should we do A or B? And I'm like, I don't know, I have to eat lunch, let's do B. And that ends up being the most consequential thing ever."

— Dario Amodei
"I actually believe it could be the case that dictatorships become morally obsolete. They become morally unworkable forms of government. And that the crisis that creates is sufficient to force us to find another way."

— Dario Amodei

Each step is meaningfully different. Each requires a different threshold of model capability. And we are, he argues, traversing the spectrum very quickly — not arriving at the final step tomorrow, but not standing still either.

The question of whether models can learn on the job — building the accumulated context that makes a six-month employee more valuable than a day-one hire — gets a more nuanced treatment. Amodei thinks this may simply not be a real barrier. Pre-training generalization already gives models broader knowledge than any individual human possesses. In-context learning at a million tokens represents weeks of human reading. And continual learning, as an engineering problem rather than a research problem, is likely solvable within one to two years through longer context windows and better inference serving. More importantly, he argues that history in ML is filled with apparent barriers that simply dissolved into the big blob of compute — syntactic versus semantic understanding, statistical correlation versus reasoning, and so on.

The Country of Geniuses and the Spreadsheet Problem

Amodei's working target for "country of geniuses in a data center" — a phrase from his essay "Machines of Loving Grace" — is 2026 or 2027, with one to two years of uncertainty on either side. At that point, AI systems would have the ability to navigate any digital interface a human can use, with intellectual capabilities matching or exceeding Nobel laureates, and the ability to interface with the physical world.

Patel presses the obvious tension: if you genuinely believe that, why isn't Anthropic buying trillions of dollars of compute right now? The answer is a lesson in operating under extreme uncertainty. Even if the technology arrives exactly when Amodei expects, the revenue curve is less certain. If a pharmaceutical company uses AI to discover a new drug, the regulatory process, manufacturing scale-up, and distribution still take years. If you buy the compute for a world where the revenue arrives simultaneously with the capability, and revenue is off by a year, "there's no force on earth that could stop you from going bankrupt." The industry as a whole is building toward multiple trillions of dollars of annual compute by 2028–2029 — roughly 10–15 gigawatts of capacity this year, growing 3x annually. Anthropic is inside that figure, buying what allows them to capture strong upside scenarios without assuming the maximum possible demand materializes on schedule.

On profitability, the model is more elegant than it appears. Each individual deployed model is already profitable at the gross margin level — inference revenue minus inference cost exceeds 50 percent. The company loses money because each training run for the next-generation model costs multiples of what the previous generation cost. As the training scale-up rate stabilizes, the underlying profitability of the business will emerge. Projected 2028 profitability is not evidence of timidity; it is a consequence of prediction error in a hypergrowth environment, where the company will swing between under- and over-provisioned states depending on whether they guessed demand correctly twelve months earlier.

Governance at the Speed of Capability

The safety and governance questions get the same intellectual honesty. Amodei is not reassured by current regulatory activity. The Tennessee bill prohibiting AI from providing emotional support struck him as legislators reacting to surface-level fears without understanding the technology. More troubling was the proposed federal moratorium on state AI regulation — a ten-year pause on state action with no credible plan for federal regulation to fill the gap. "Given the serious dangers I lay out around biological weapons and bioterrorism, autonomy risk, and the timelines we've been talking about — ten years is an eternity. That's a crazy thing to do."

His preferred approach: start with transparency standards now. As risks become more concrete and better-evidenced — which he thinks could happen as early as later this year — move quickly to targeted regulation in the specific domains where risk has materialized. The process must be nimble in a way that legislatures normally are not, which is part of why he is writing public essays, speaking to policymakers directly, and flagging urgency as loudly as he can.

On geopolitics, his concern is about initial conditions. He does not believe one country should dictate the rules of the post-AGI world. But he does believe there will be a window — possibly narrow — where some countries or coalitions have reached critical thresholds of AI capability before others, and that the rules negotiated during that window will shape what comes after. His preference is that democratic nations hold stronger hands at that table, which is why he advocates for export controls on chips to China despite the revenue pressure against it. Growth and economic value, he argues, will come easily in an AI-accelerated world. Distribution of benefits, political freedom, and rights are the things that will actually be hard.

The Asymmetry of Retrospect

The conversation closes with an unusual question: when someone eventually writes the history of this era, what will they most likely miss? Amodei's answer cuts to the heart of his concern. They will miss the genuine contingency of it all — the degree to which, at every point along the exponential, the people inside did not know it was inevitable. "When people look back, it will be hard for them to put themselves in the place of people who were actually making a bet on this thing to happen that wasn't inevitable." The world outside those bets was not acting on them at all. If history renders this period as obvious, it will have gotten the phenomenology completely wrong.

The second thing they will miss is the speed. The decisions that turn out to be most consequential may not have been recognized as consequential when they were made. "Some very critical decision will be some decision that someone just comes into my office and says, Dario, you have two minutes — should we do A or B? And I'm like, I don't know, I have to eat lunch, let's do B. And that ends up being the most consequential thing ever."

That admission — from the CEO of one of the most important companies in the world at what may be one of the most important moments in history — is not false modesty. It is the most honest thing he says.

What To Do With This

  • Treat the 10x-per-year revenue curve as a planning constraint, not a headline. Amodei's framework for data center purchasing — buy enough to capture strong upside without betting on the maximum possible scenario — applies to any organization making multi-year AI infrastructure commitments. Model the demand cone, not just the capability curve.
  • Track AI's productivity contribution as a percentage of total factor output, not lines of code. Amodei estimates coding agents are currently delivering a 15–20% total factor speedup — up from ~5% six months ago. This is the metric that actually predicts competitive advantage, not adoption rates or lines-of-code percentages.
  • Reframe "on-the-job learning" as a context-window engineering problem, not a fundamental barrier. For most knowledge-work tasks, the accumulated context a six-month employee has built can be approximated through a combination of broad pre-training and large context windows. Before assuming a task requires human continuity, test whether stuffing the model's context window with all relevant history achieves comparable results.
  • Build RL training environments across the broadest realistic distribution of task variations, not just the most common scenarios. Amodei's analogy to pre-training generalization is actionable: narrow training (like GPT-1 on fan fiction) produces narrow capability. If you are building agents for a domain, the goal is breadth of scenario coverage, not depth on the median case. Labelbox's approach — building thousands of distinct interaction scenarios across multiple persona types — is the right pattern.
  • Accelerate your own AI adoption now specifically because enterprise procurement cycles are long. Amodei's observation that large enterprises are adopting Claude Code significantly faster than previous technologies while still taking months to complete is a competitive signal. If you are at an enterprise that has not started the legal and security review process for a serious AI deployment, the time cost of waiting is compounding against you.
  • Watch the "verifiable task" frontier as the leading indicator of general capability. Amodei is highly confident about AI performance on tasks with objective metrics (coding, math) and views the gap to non-verifiable tasks (strategic planning, scientific discovery) as closing through generalization. Monitor progress on verifiable benchmarks — particularly end-to-end software engineering tasks — as the clearest signal of when broader capability thresholds are being crossed.
  • Read "Machines of Loving Grace" and "The Adolescence of Technology" as operating frameworks, not thought experiments. These essays contain Amodei's actual probability-weighted views on timelines, governance, geopolitics, and economic impact. They are being shared with policymakers and should inform any organization thinking about AI strategy over a two-to-five year horizon.
  • Build internal communication infrastructure for the pace of change, not for quarterly update cycles. Amodei speaks to Anthropic's 2,500 employees every two weeks with a three-to-four page document covering model launches, product developments, competitive landscape, and geopolitics — then takes live questions. The alternative (filtering everything through six levels of management) destroys organizational coherence under conditions of rapid change. Adopt a similar cadence if you are leading a team through significant AI-driven transformation.
  • Engage now on transparency-first AI regulation before the policy window closes. Amodei's sequencing — transparency standards now, targeted safety regulation as specific risks materialize — is the most actionable near-term policy position. Organizations that want to shape rather than react to AI regulation should be engaged at the transparency standard stage, not waiting for the more restrictive legislation that follows.

Lines Worth Keeping

"The most surprising thing has been the lack of public recognition of how close we are to the end of the exponential. To me, it is absolutely wild that you have people talking about these same tired old hot-button political issues — and around us, we're near the end of the exponential."

— Dario Amodei
"I'm at like 90% on that. It's hard to go much higher than 90% because the world is so unpredictable. Maybe the irreducible uncertainty is things like multiple companies have internal turmoil, Taiwan gets invaded, and all the fabs get blown up by missiles."

— Dario Amodei, on his confidence that AGI arrives within 10 years
"There is zero time for bullshit. There is zero time for feeling like we're productive when we're not. Like, we see the end productivity every few months in the form of model launches. There's no kidding yourself about this."

— Dario Amodei, on productivity measurement inside Anthropic
"I think everything we've seen so far is compatible with the idea that there's one fast exponential that's the capability of the model, and then there's another fast exponential that's downstream of that, which is the diffusion of the model into the economy. Not instant, not slow, much faster than any previous technology, but it has its limits."

— Dario Amodei
"What will not come easily is distribution of benefits, distribution of wealth, political freedom. These are the things that are going to be hard to achieve. When I think about policy, I think the technology and the market will deliver all the fundamental benefits almost faster than we can take them."

— Dario Amodei
"When people look back, it will be hard for them to put themselves in the place of people who were actually making a bet on this thing to happen that wasn't inevitable — that there's a world outside us that's not acting on that at all. And that's one of the things I'm trying to change."

— Dario Amodei
"Some very critical decision will be some decision that someone just comes into my office and says, Dario, you have two minutes — should we do A or B? And I'm like, I don't know, I have to eat lunch, let's do B. And that ends up being the most consequential thing ever."

— Dario Amodei
"I actually believe it could be the case that dictatorships become morally obsolete. They become morally unworkable forms of government. And that the crisis that creates is sufficient to force us to find another way."

— Dario Amodei

item.title: The Delegation Threshold: How OpenAI's Codex Lead Thinks About the Future of Building
item.description:
The Delegation Threshold: How OpenAI's Codex Lead Thinks About the Future of Building

Something shifted in December. Not a gradual drift, but a step function. According to Alexander Embiricos, Product Lead for Codex at OpenAI, the release of GPT-4o Codex marked the moment engineers at OpenAI stopped pairing with AI and started delegating to it. Before that inflection point, you still needed your hands near the keyboard. You were pair programming — the model handled small pieces while you steered. After it, engineers stopped opening IDEs at all. They would sketch a spec, align on a plan, and let the agent cook.

That distinction — pairing versus delegating — is the organizing idea behind everything Embiricos thinks about product, competition, enterprise adoption, and the future of work. It sounds subtle. It isn't. The entire Codex app was built around it, designed without a text editor precisely because including one would have muddied the message: you are here to manage agents and review their output, not to write lines yourself.

The Bottleneck Isn't the Model. It's You.

Embiricos has a contrarian thesis about what is actually slowing down the adoption of AI: it isn't compute, it isn't model capability, and it isn't architecture. The binding constraint is human typing speed and the cognitive overhead of knowing when and how to ask for help.

The logic is disarmingly simple. Most people use AI a few dozen times a day. But if the friction were zero — no prompting required, no creative effort to identify where AI could help — AI could be intervening thousands of times a day on your behalf. "I think AI should be helping us tens of thousands of times per day," he said. The gap between thirty and thirty thousand isn't a model problem. It's a product problem.

His proposed solution: stop thinking in terms of use-case-specific vertical applications and instead build open-ended tools that let curious users explore. The genius of Claude Code's original launch, in his assessment, was that it put a capable agent directly in your terminal and let you figure out what to do with it. No predetermined workflow. No constrained task list. Just a tool and a prompt box. That openness allowed users to develop their own fluency, and fluency is what ultimately drives adoption beyond the power-user tier.

This puts Embiricos in direct disagreement with the enterprise-first, field-deployment-engineer approach. His argument isn't that FDEs are useless — he's actively hiring them — but that top-down workflow automation without bottom-up user fluency creates a dangerous disconnect. If an employee has never used ChatGPT but suddenly finds their job being automated by an LLM, they have no mental model for what's happening, no ability to steer it, and no sense of agency. In contrast, if that same employee has been using AI in their daily work, they arrive at automation as participants rather than subjects.

Three Phases, Speed-Run

Embiricos lays out a clean three-phase framework for how Codex — and agentic AI more broadly — should expand beyond software engineering:

Phase one: Build agents that are really good at coding, because that is where LLMs are demonstrably capable and where product-market fit exists today.

Phase two: Recognize that using a computer is universally valuable, and that coding is actually the best mechanism for any agent to interact with a computer. Take the same flexible, open-ended approach and make it available to anyone who wants to tinker — not just engineers.

Phase three: Once you have signal from real usage patterns, productize the specific workflows that have demonstrated value. Build highly specific features that work out of the box. Kill the exploration overhead.

He believes this entire arc will compress into months, not years. The Codex app's launch — and its reception from non-engineers building simple things through it — is already demonstrating the beginning of Phase Two.

On Code Review, Trust, and the Real Bottleneck in Code Generation

Code generation, Embiricos says, has become "basically trivial." The hard problem is now verification. How do you know the code is good? How do you trust an agent to own an entire microservice or internal tool and iterate based on user feedback without human review at each step?

His practical answer for today: have Codex review its own pull requests. This sounds circular, but it works precisely because the model has been trained to produce high-signal feedback with very few false positives. When Codex flags something, you can trust it. Nearly all code at OpenAI is now automatically reviewed by Codex when pushed to any significant repository.

There is also an underappreciated shift in what "review" means in an agentic workflow. The spec and the plan have become more important than the code itself. Codex now ships a "plan mode" where the agent proposes its approach, asks clarifying questions, and waits for alignment before writing a line. This mirrors the request-for-comments process that experienced engineering teams use with new hires. You are no longer reviewing the code. You are reviewing the architecture decision upstream of it.

The longer-term ambition is an agent you can trust to own an entire loop — writing, deploying, monitoring, receiving user feedback, and iterating — without human intervention at each gate. That requires solving both an intelligence problem and a safety and controls problem simultaneously. Embiricos is candid that this is hard and under-invested-in across the industry.

What Makes Good Interfaces for Agents Also Makes Good Interfaces for Humans

One of the more practically useful observations Embiricos offers: the best interfaces for agents to do their work tend to be the best interfaces for humans too. His example is test output. Most test runners, naively configured, emit the output of every test — passing and failing. Filtering to emit only failed tests is better for humans who have to diagnose problems. It also turns out to be dramatically better for agents parsing that same output. The optimization is identical.

This has an important implication for teams thinking about "AI-proofing" their codebases: you do not need a separate agent-optimization pass. Simply ask whether your codebase is easy for a human to work with. If the answer is yes, it's probably already better for agents too. If the answer is no, that's your priority regardless of whether a human or an agent is doing the work.

The Market Structure Question: Fewer Winners Than You Think

Harry Stebbings pressed hard on the competitive landscape — Cursor, Claude Code, Codex — and Embiricos offered a view that is more consolidating than the current plurality of tools might suggest.

His argument: we are in a temporary phase where agents are demonstrably good primarily at coding. Other domains — genuine knowledge work, complex cross-functional tasks — haven't yet produced the same product-market fit. But that's a temporary condition, not a permanent one. As agents become capable across domains, the right product architecture is a single agent you can talk to about anything, paired with specialized UI for deep functional work when needed.

The Dropbox-versus-Slack analogy he reaches for is instructive. It was objectively more efficient to comment on a document in Dropbox at the relevant timestamp than to discuss it in Slack. But Slack was the center of gravity for communication, so everything migrated there even at a cost to optimality. An agent that becomes the center of gravity for work — the thing employees are trained to go to for anything — will accrue that same gravitational pull. Multi-agent sprawl, where employees have to figure out which of twelve agents handles which domain, prevents fluency and therefore prevents deep adoption. Embiricos's bet is on concentration.

Where Embiricos Would and Would Not Invest

Asked directly to advise on the SaaS durability question — are software companies facing existential displacement from model providers? — Embiricos offered a clear framework. The question is not whether a company builds software. The question is whether it owns either of two durable assets: a direct relationship with a human user, or a system of record.

Companies that own both are likely fine. Companies that are a "glue layer" — software that orchestrates other systems but owns neither the user relationship nor the underlying data — are the ones he would be nervous about. He does not think Salesforce's 30% decline is warranted. He does think customer support software faces genuine structural risk.

His positive investment thesis: physical infrastructure (energy, hardware) and gnarly, relationship-intensive markets — fintech with complex bank integrations, regulated industries, sectors where knowing the customer is itself the competitive advantage. "I don't think OpenAI is going to go into building 500 relationships with banks in Southeast Asia," he said. That observation, more than any framework, captures the actual logic.

He also flagged a shift in the founder profile worth backing. For a period, you could invest in someone who was simply good at building product, because building was hard. That's less true now. The scarce thing is domain expertise, distribution thinking, and a genuine thesis about a specific customer. Those founder qualities have returned to primacy.

For the Engineering Student Watching

Embiricos was asked what advice he'd give a computer science student entering the workforce. His answer was deliberately optimistic and practical: there has never been a better time to be an engineer. Ramping into a complex codebase that would have taken weeks can now be compressed dramatically because you can interrogate the codebase directly through an AI. The tooling is extraordinary.

But the thing that becomes scarcer precisely because building is easier is agency, taste, and demonstrated quality. His hiring signal is not a polished resume. It is someone who wrote to him with an interesting thought and a link to an interesting project they built. The signal is initiative and the ability to make something worth sharing. That calculus will not change as AI gets better. If anything, the stakes of demonstrating those qualities will rise.

What To Do With This

  • Implement automatic AI code review on every push to your primary branches. Embiricos notes Codex has been explicitly trained for high-signal code review with low false positives — meaning you can trust its flags. Set it up to run automatically rather than waiting for developers to remember to invoke it manually.
  • Audit your test output configuration and filter to failed tests only. Embiricos's specific example: most test runners emit everything by default, which is noise for humans and terrible for agents. Changing your test runner to surface only failures improves both human debugging speed and AI agent effectiveness simultaneously.
  • Introduce plan-mode reviews before large agent-written code blocks. Before letting an agent write significant code, require it to propose its approach and ask clarifying questions. Treat this like a request-for-comments or architecture review. This is where human judgment has the highest leverage now that code generation itself is largely solved.
  • Create an agents.md file in your repositories. This is an emerging open standard (now used by nearly every major coding agent except Claude) for giving persistent, repo-level instructions to any agent working in your codebase. Codex, Amp, and others read from it. Investing an hour to write one now means every future agent interaction benefits from your preferences without re-prompting.
  • Move your AI adoption metric from weekly active to daily active users. Embiricos openly acknowledged this in the conversation — weekly active was a convenience standard inherited from early days, but as AI becomes an IDE replacement, daily active is the right frequency. If you are tracking AI adoption inside your organization, switch the denominator now before the metric calcifies.
  • Evaluate your product or business against the two durability tests: user relationship ownership and system-of-record ownership. If you own neither — if you are a coordination or orchestration layer that sits between other systems — treat that as a genuine strategic risk and reorient toward acquiring one of the two durable assets, not just optimizing the product.
  • When hiring for product roles in AI-heavy environments, weight for domain expertise and distribution thinking over pure product craft. Embiricos's argument is that building is increasingly commoditized, making "good at building" insufficient as a hiring signal. Interview for: does this person have a specific, researched thesis about a customer problem? Do they think seriously about how a product reaches people?
  • Resist over-productizing AI tools too early for your organization. The pattern Embiricos describes repeatedly — top-down workflow automation before bottom-up user fluency — produces underutilization and disempowerment. Give employees access to general-purpose tools first. Let them develop muscle memory. Then ladder in automation around the workflows they have already started pulling AI into on their own.
  • If you are building an AI startup, prioritize granular, gnarly markets over horizontal coordination plays. Embiricos's specific investment guidance: energy infrastructure, Southeast Asian banking relationships, regulated industries where market knowledge is the moat. These are the spaces where model providers are unlikely to compete directly because the value is in accumulated relationship capital, not in the intelligence layer.

Lines Worth Keeping

"I think AI should be helping us tens of thousands of times per day. The problem is I'm too lazy to type out that many prompts, and I'm too uncreative to figure out all the ways that AI can help me."

— Alexander Embiricos

"CodeGen — writing code — has become basically trivial now. But the hard part is what you were talking about with code review. How do we know the code quality is good? How do we know we're doing the right things? Those bottlenecks are underappreciated and under-invested in."

— Alexander Embiricos

"Because it's never been easier to build things, the thing that becomes scarcer is agency, taste, and quality."

— Alexander Embiricos

"If you build our agent purely as workflow automation, then it's always going to be pulling teeth to get that thing started. You're going to need to hire Accenture or someone to come in. They're going to deploy FTEs. It's going to be tough. But if you can build a system that people just love using, even if they only use it for partial tasks, over time they'll get better and better at using it."

— Alexander Embiricos

"Slack is just such a center of gravity of people talking to each other. Nobody wants to comment on the document. I just want to Slack you. I think we're going to see something similar at work where if there is a single agent you can use for nearly anything, there will just be this giant pull."

— Alexander Embiricos

"Does this SaaS company own a relationship with a human on the other end of things? And if it does, then I suspect it's not going away. Or does the SaaS company own some really important system of record? It's probably not going away. Maybe both of those two things are more important than ever, actually."

— Alexander Embiricos

"The best interfaces for Codex to do work also tend to be the best interfaces for humans. So when people ask how can I make my codebase more efficient for the agent to work with, the answer is often: well, have you looked at it yourself? And is it easy for a human to work with?"

— Alexander Embiricos

"One of my darkest moments while running the startup was recognizing that I had spent the last few months trying to avoid losing. All of a sudden I was like, oh my god, that is why I'm so unhappy. And that's probably why this startup isn't going well."

— Alexander Embiricos
